1:
The verse opens the disputed but treasured story of the woman taken in adultery, and the witnesses begin with the Mount of Olives. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place and the reason: it lay about a mile east of Jerusalem (Barnes), and Jesus withdrew there, Gill says, “partly to decline the danger… in the night season,” partly to lodge with “his dear friends Lazarus, Martha, and Mary” nearby at Bethany (so Robertson too), “and chiefly for private prayer.” Lightfoot, uncertain whether he went to Bethany or “to some booth fixed in that mount,” recalls that the festival crowds pitched tents outside the walls within a sabbath day’s journey (Deuteronomy 16:7). Henry, for the Puritans, reads pathos into it: perhaps Jesus “had no friend there… courageous enough to give him a night’s lodging,” so that while his persecutors “had houses of their own to go to” (John 7:53), he must go “a mile or two out of town.”

The Latin fathers, through the Catena, find a mystery in the place. Augustine asks, “where ought Christ to teach, except on the mount of Olives — the mount of ointment, the mount of chrism?” For “the name Christ is from chrism… He has anointed us, for wrestling with the devil.” Alcuin develops it: the olive, “in the Greek signifying pity,” marks “the height of our Lord’s pity,” and as oil “floats above all other liquids,” so the Psalmist says, “Your mercy is over all Your works” (Psalm 145:9) — a fitting seat for the mercy he is about to show.

Ellicott raises, in passing, the question that hangs over the whole section: “the Mount of Olives is nowhere mentioned by St. John,” whose habit is to explain Palestinian places “for his Greek readers,” a small sign among many that this paragraph stands apart from the surrounding Gospel. The verse thus opens a narrative the church has long loved, even as the commentators begin to weigh whether John’s own pen set it here.

The witnesses note, too, that the verse belongs with the close of the previous chapter, where “every man went unto his own house” (John 7:53) — Ellicott observing how “the artificial division into chapters often mars the sense.” The contrast is pointed: the rulers retire to their homes, the homeless Teacher to a hillside; and from that mount of prayer and pity he will come down in the morning to meet, with mercy, the very accusers his enemies are even now preparing.
2:
“And early in the morning he came again into the temple, and all the people came unto him; and he sat down, and taught them.” The witnesses, all grammatical-historical here, read a vivid morning scene. Gill marks Christ’s “diligence, constancy, and assiduity in his ministerial work, as well as his courage and intrepidity,” returning fearlessly though “careful to give them no advantage against him, before his time.” Ellicott notes the picture “agrees with His custom during the week preceding the Crucifixion” (compare Luke 21:37–38, where Jesus teaches by day in the temple and lodges by night on the mount). Robertson lingers on the grammar: “early in the morning” is orthros, daybreak (a word John never uses, preferring prōi); the imperfect “all the people came” pictures “the enthusiasm of the whole crowd now, as opposed to the divisions in chapter 7”; and the participle “he sat down” (kathisas, ingressive) with the inchoative imperfect “began to teach” paints the settling of the Teacher into his seat before the throng.

The textual question surfaces again in the detail. Ellicott observes that the words “and he sat down and taught them” are absent from the Cambridge manuscript, “the oldest authority for the section,” and Robertson grants that, “if the paragraph is genuine,” the time is the day after the feast, but “if not genuine, there is no way of telling the time of this apparently true incident.” Even the commentators most cautious about the passage’s pedigree, the witnesses show, treat the scene it records as a true and characteristic morning in the temple courts — the eager people gathered, the seated Teacher, the calm before the accusers break in.

The setting matters for what follows. The Teacher is seated, the people gathered and eager, the temple court full — and it is into this open, public scene that the accusers will thrust the woman, choosing the most crowded and shameful stage they can find. The witnesses thus see in the quiet of the morning teaching a deliberate foil to the violence about to interrupt it, and a reminder that Christ’s habit, even under threat, was simply to teach the people who came to him at first light.
3:
“And the scribes and Pharisees brought unto him a woman taken in adultery; and when they had set her in the midst…” Two questions occupy the witnesses: the authenticity of the passage, and the malice of the accusers. On the first, Calvin gives the classic balanced verdict for the Reformed: “it is plain enough that this passage was unknown anciently to the Greek Churches, and some conjecture that it has been brought from some other place and inserted here. But as it has always been received by the Latin Churches, and is found in many old Greek manuscripts, and contains nothing unworthy of an Apostolic Spirit, there is no reason why we should refuse to apply it to our advantage.” The grammatical-historical commentators share the doubt while valuing the story: Ellicott notes that “the scribes” are “never named by St. John,” whose word for the hierarchy is “the Jews” — another mark that the section sits loosely in this Gospel.

On the malice, the witnesses are united. Robertson calls the bringing of the woman “in itself an unlawful thing to do,” since “they had a court for the trial of such a case” (Dods); their dramatic present “bring” and their setting her “in the midst,” where “all could see her,” betray that the trial is staged for Jesus, not for her — “they knew his proneness to forgive sinners.” Gill exposes their hypocrisy: they acted “not because of their abhorrence… of the sin,” for by their own traditions they had all but made the law void, requiring a formal admonition before any penalty. Lightfoot adds the dark background that “since the increase of adultery, the bitter waters” of Numbers 5 had been abolished, the Sanhedrin (perhaps the very men present) having abandoned the old trial because the sin was so common — and Clarke notes that adultery was “so common that they had ceased to put the law in force against it.” The accusers thus drag a guilty woman before the Teacher of grace not to honour Moses but to bait a trap.

Robertson adds that by setting her “in the midst,” where everyone could watch, the accusers meant to force a public verdict and so make Christ’s answer the more compromising; the woman’s exposure is calculated, not incidental. The witnesses agree that the deepest irony of the scene is already present: those who profess to defend the law of Moses break it in the very act of invoking it — arraigning a prisoner outside the lawful court, and trading on a woman’s shame to ruin the one teacher in Israel who would not despise her.
4:
“They say unto him, Master, this woman was taken in adultery, in the very act.” The witnesses read the address and the charge as parts of a calculated trap. The grammatical-historical commentators note the show of courtesy: they call him “Master,” Gill says, “in a handsome and respectful manner, the better to cover their ill design.” Robertson presses the legal language: “hath been taken” is the perfect kateilēptai, “caught and still guilty”; “in the very act” renders ep’ autophōrō, an old idiom (literally “caught in the act of theft,” extended to any crime) found nowhere else in the Greek Bible. The detail of the witnessed act, the commentators agree, is meant to make the case airtight and the law’s demand unanswerable — there can be no question of the facts, only of the sentence, which is exactly the ground on which they hope to snare him.

Ellicott records the variant of the Cambridge manuscript, the section’s oldest authority, which here reads “the priests say unto Him, tempting Him, that they might have to accuse Him” — naming the accusers as priests and bringing forward at once the statement of their motive that the Received text holds to v.6. Whichever the wording, the witnesses agree the verse exposes the accusers more than the accused: they parade a woman’s shame “in the midst,” feigning reverence for Christ and for the Law, while their real aim is to use her as bait. The very precision with which they state the case — caught, in adultery, in the act — betrays that their concern is not her sin but his answer, and that even her public humiliation is, to them, merely a means to an end.

The witnesses observe, too, the asymmetry the law itself condemned: Moses required the death of both the adulterer and the adulteress (Leviticus 20:10), yet only the woman is dragged before Christ, the man left unnamed and unpunished. Whatever the explanation, the omission underscores that the accusers’ zeal was selective and their aim ulterior. They had not come to purge Israel of adultery but to corner its Teacher; the woman, caught and shamed, was to them no more than the convenient occasion of the trap.
5:
“Now Moses in the law commanded us, that such should be stoned: but what sayest thou?” The witnesses give much care to the legal precision, and then to the snare. The grammatical-historical commentators note that stoning was not the general penalty for adultery: the law simply commanded death (Leviticus 20:10; Deuteronomy 22:22) without specifying the manner, and the rabbis took strangulation as the default where the mode was unnamed (Gill, Clarke, Barnes). Stoning was prescribed for a particular case — a betrothed virgin who proved false (Deuteronomy 22:23–24) — so Ellicott and Lightfoot infer the woman was either “an Israelitess… betrothed only,” or a married priest’s daughter, the Talmud distinguishing the penalties precisely (a betrothed daughter of Israel stoned, a married priest’s daughter stoned, a betrothed priest’s daughter burnt). Robertson agrees the case “may have strictly come” under the stoning rule.

But the legal nicety, all agree, is a pretext. The real force lies in “but what sayest thou?” — “a snare,” Ellicott says, “in which they hope to take Him whatever His answer,” like the question about the tribute money (Matthew 22:17). Gill marks that they cited “Moses,” feigning reverence for the very law their traditions had hollowed out. The dilemma they set is sharp: should Jesus uphold the death sentence, he would seem to usurp the power of life and death the Romans had withdrawn from the Jews, and to deny the mercy he preached; should he set it aside, he would appear to “teach contrary to Moses” and condone the sin. They suppose there is no third way — and so present a guilty woman as the instrument of their plot, careless of her shame so long as they snare her Judge.

The witnesses also weigh the broader irony of the appeal to Moses. The same men who in chapter 7 had been charged by Christ that “none of you keepeth the law” (John 7:19) now wield that law as a weapon, professing a reverence for it their own lives belied. By forcing the choice between Moses and mercy they thought to leave Jesus no escape; yet, the commentators note, they reckoned without the answer he was about to give, which would honour both the law’s holiness and the sinner’s hope.
6:
“This they said, tempting him, that they might have to accuse him. But Jesus stooped down, and with his finger wrote on the ground, as though he heard them not.” The witnesses lay bare both the trap and the famous gesture. On the trap, the grammatical-historical commentators spell out the dilemma: if Jesus condemned her, Clarke explains, “they might have accused him to Pilate, as arrogating… the power of life and death, which the Romans had taken away from the Jews” (and Roman law did not even make adultery capital); if he acquitted her, “they might have represented him… as one who decided contrary to the law.” Barnes adds that they likely expected him to side against the law, as in the Sabbath disputes, and so discredit himself. Robertson notes the present participle “tempting” in its “evil sense,” and that “laying of traps for Jesus was a common practice of his enemies,” now grown into “a habit.”

On the writing, the witnesses confess uncertainty and offer competing readings. Ellicott insists “any inquiry as to what He wrote is excluded by the fact that the narrative would certainly have recorded it had it been known”; the meaning lies “in the symbolism of the action.” Several manuscripts and fathers could not resist a guess — some copies add that he wrote “the sins of each one of them” (Clarke, Ellicott) — and Lightfoot ties the gesture to the trial of the suspected wife by the bitter water (Numbers 5), where dust from the temple floor was used. Calvin, for the Reformed, declines all such conjectures: by stooping Christ “intended to show that he despised them,” as one who “draws lines on the wall” to signal he is not attending; he expressly rejects even Augustine’s elegant idea that Christ wrote on earth, not stone (Exodus 31:18), to mark the difference of Law and Gospel. Wesley reads it more gently: “by this silent action he fixed their wandering, hurrying thoughts, in order to awaken their consciences,” and “signified that he was not then come to condemn but to save the world.”
7:
“So when they continued asking him, he lifted up himself, and said unto them, He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone at her.” The witnesses dwell on “without sin” and on the law of the witnesses. The grammatical-historical commentators take “without sin” (anamartētos) not absolutely but “of the class of sins of which her sin was an instance” (Ellicott) — “the same kind of sin, adultery, fornication” (Clarke), as Barnes and Wesley agree. Of the immorality among the rulers that gave the word its sting, Ellicott notes, “evidence is not wanting.” The command that the sinless cast “the first stone” draws on the Mosaic rule that “the hands of the witnesses shall be first upon him” to put him to death (Deuteronomy 17:6–7); Barnes and Clarke describe the grim procedure — the witnesses casting the culprit down and hurling the first stone — so that, Calvin says, “greater caution might be used in bearing testimony.” Robertson pictures the moment: Jesus seemed embarrassed, but as much “because of the brazen hardness of the prosecutors as because of the shame of the deed,” then “lifted himself up” to give “an answer of which they think not.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks both the likeness and the difference between Christ’s words and the Law. The Law required the witnesses’ own hands “that greater caution might be used”; Christ presses the principle inward, so that this “very argument had weight with those slanderers, desperate as they were,” for “no sooner do they obtain a sight of it, than they lay aside those fierce passions.” Yet Christ does not, the witnesses insist, abolish the magistrate’s right to punish sin; he turns the accusers’ own consciences upon them, demanding that those who would execute the law be themselves clear of the very crime they prosecute. The single sentence thus disarms the trap without denying the law — and leaves every accuser to judge himself.

The witnesses underline that Christ’s reply neither acquits the crime nor accepts the accusers’ standing to prosecute it. He grants the law its force — let the stoning proceed — but fixes the first stone in a hand that must be clean of the same sin, and so exposes the prosecutors’ unfitness without ever pronouncing the woman innocent. It is, the commentators agree, a masterstroke that turns the trial inward: the men who came to judge are made to stand at the bar of their own consciences, and the law they invoked becomes the mirror of their guilt.
8:
“And again he stooped down, and wrote on the ground.” The witnesses read the repeated gesture as deliberate mercy and quiet pressure. The grammatical-historical commentators agree it was not embarrassment but design. Gill says that, “having said enough to confound them, and yet unwilling to pursue the matter any further, or publicly expose them,” Christ stooped again “that they might have an opportunity of withdrawing themselves without any further notice of his.” Ellicott reads it the same way: “the repeated action repeats His determination to avoid the office of judge. He has answered them, and He leaves His answer to do its work. There is a law written in their hearts, and this, while He now writes on the ground, is convicting them.” Robertson notes the variation in the Greek — the rare compound katakypsas for “stooped down,” and that “with his finger” is not in the best text — calling it simply “the second picture of Jesus writing on the ground.”

The same impulse to explain the writing that marked v.6 appears again: Ellicott records “a strange addition at the end of the verse, in one of the older MSS… ‘and wrote on the ground the sin of each one of them,’” a copyist’s attempt to give the action a definite sense. But the witnesses agree the point lies not in the unknown words but in the posture: by looking away, Christ spares the accusers his gaze and lets conscience do its work, so that they may retire of their own accord rather than be driven out. The mercy he is about to show the woman he shows first, in this small averting of his eyes, even toward the men who came to trap him.

The witnesses see in the double stooping a studied restraint. Christ might have answered word for word, exposing each accuser by name, but instead he writes again and waits, giving the convicted men room to leave with what dignity remained to them. This averting of his gaze is itself an act of the mercy that governs the whole scene — the same mercy that will spare the woman now first spares her accusers the public shaming he could have inflicted, and lets their own consciences, not his tongue, drive them out.
9:
“And they which heard it, being convicted by their own conscience, went out one by one, beginning at the eldest, even unto the last: and Jesus was left alone, and the woman standing in the midst.” The witnesses describe the silent rout of the accusers. The grammatical-historical commentators identify “they which heard it” as “the Scribes and Pharisees,” not the disciples or the crowd (Gill), and read the order of departure with care: “beginning at the eldest” means, Barnes and Clarke agree, “from those who were in highest reputation to the lowest in rank,” or, as Robertson and Dods put it, “from the elder men, as was natural, for they had more sins of this sort which they recalled” — “they are summoned to judge themselves rather than the woman.” Lightfoot calls the result “a thing little less than miracle”: Christ “had determined to shame these wicked men before the common people,” and his word carried “that peculiar force… that they could not stand it out,” but “with shame and confusion” confessed their guilt by retreating.

On the closing clause the commentators guard against a misreading. “Jesus was left alone” does not mean the temple emptied, but that the accusers were gone; the woman stood “in the midst” of the crowd that still surrounded the Teacher (Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), as the discourse that immediately follows (v.12) shows. Ellicott notes that “being convicted by their own conscience” is probably a copyist’s explanatory addition, “the meaning quite clear without it.” The scene the witnesses paint is stark: the law’s self-appointed executioners, undone by their own consciences, slip away eldest first, leaving the sinner alone with the only One entitled to condemn her — who will not.

The witnesses draw the moral of the rout. The accusers came confident in the law and in the woman’s guilt, and left convicted by the one judge they could not silence — their own conscience. That the eldest withdrew first, Robertson and Dods note, is fitting, for age had only lengthened the tally of remembered sins; the very seniority that lent them authority lent them also the heavier burden of self-knowledge. So the trial they staged against Christ ends as a trial of themselves, and the crowd they meant to sway watches their silent confession of defeat.
10:
“When Jesus had lifted up himself, and saw none but the woman, he said unto her, Woman, where are those thine accusers? hath no man condemned thee?” The witnesses, all grammatical-historical here, mark the gentleness and the aim of the question. Robertson pictures the moment: “Jesus had kept on writing on the ground as the accusers had slipped away one by one,” and now lifts himself to find them gone — “no one dared to cast a stone at the woman on Christ’s terms.” Barnes is careful about the sense of “condemn”: Jesus had bidden any innocent man to cast a stone and so “condemn her,” using “the power… to condemn her”; none had done so — “they had accused her, but they had not proceeded to the act expressive of judicial condemnation.” Wesley glosses it the same way: “hath no judicial sentence been passed upon thee?”

Ellicott hears in the question a purpose beyond the legal: “He has spoken words which have carried a lesson to them; he has now a lesson for her.” The question — “Of all who brought the charge against thee, was there not one to condemn thee?” — is “put to her to lead to thoughts of her sin,” turning her from the terror of the accusers to the searching of her own heart. The Cambridge manuscript, Ellicott notes, has the shorter “Where are they?” Either way the witnesses agree that Christ, now alone with the woman in the midst of the people, neither ignores her guilt nor presses the accusers’ advantage, but prepares her, by a tender question, for the word of mercy and command that follows.

The witnesses note the dignity Christ shows the woman even now. He does not lecture her before the crowd, nor rehearse her sin, but addresses her simply as “Woman” and asks a question that lets her own lips report the accusers’ collapse. The mercy that drove out the prosecutors without a word of his own now turns to the prisoner without a word of reproach, leading her gently from the dread of stoning to the quieter and deeper reckoning with her own heart that his next words will require.
11:
The climax — “She said, No man, Lord. And Jesus said unto her, Neither do I condemn thee: go, and sin no more” — draws from the witnesses one united concern: to show that Christ’s mercy does not condone the sin. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that “condemn” here is judicial. Barnes: this is “the sense of passing sentence as a magistrate, for this is what they had arraigned her for”; Jesus “claimed no civil authority” and so “did not exercise it,” yet “regarded the action… as sin.” Robertson agrees Jesus “does not condone her sin” — compare “I judge no one” (v.15) — but “does give the poor woman another chance,” the command “sin no more” (the very words spoken to the healed man in John 5:14) meaning literally “henceforth no longer go on sinning.” Ellicott observes she offers “no plea for forgiveness… no attempt at defence,” and that though there is “no expression of forgiveness or peace as we find in other cases,” yet the words that bid her go “are the condemnation of her sin” even as they spare her person (John 3:17). Clarke records, with Bishop Pearce, how the fear that “I do not condemn thee” might “countenance… that crime” led the early church to omit the story from the public lectionaries — part of how it dropped from many manuscripts.

Calvin, for the Reformed, guards the point most vigorously. Christ did not “absolutely acquit the woman, but… allowed her to go at liberty,” doing nothing outside “his office,” for he “had been sent by the Father to gather” the lost (Matthew 10:6), and so “exhorts the woman to repentance, and comforts her by a promise of grace.” Those who infer “that adultery ought not to be punished with death” reason as falsely, he says, as if Christ’s refusal to divide an inheritance (Luke 12:13) abolished property law; rather “there will be no crime whatever exempted from the penalties of the law, if adultery be not punished.” Wesley sums the consensus: “neither do I take upon me to pass any such sentence; let this deliverance lead thee to repentance.”
12:
“I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life.” The witnesses receive this as one of the great “I am” sayings, set against its festival background. The grammatical-historical commentators recover the scene: at the feast of tabernacles, in the Court of the Women where Jesus stood (v.20), great golden candelabra were lit each night for the “Rejoicing of the Water-Drawing,” recalling “the pillar of cloud by day and of fire by night” (Ellicott, Robertson). Against that blaze of lamps Jesus makes “this supreme and exclusive claim” — he had called his followers “the light of the world” (Matthew 5:14), but that was light reflected from him, while he is himself “the true light” (John 1:9; cf. Psalm 27:1; Isaiah 60:19). Lightfoot and Clarke add that the rabbis themselves named “Light” a title of the Messiah and of God (“light dwelleth with him,” Daniel 2:22), so that Christ claims for himself what they ascribed to God alone.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the claim against the foregoing scene and the chapter-7 dispute. Alcuin says that, “having absolved the woman from her sin, lest some should doubt… His power to forgive sins, He deigns to give further disclosure of His divine nature.” Bede notes he says not “the light of Angels, or of heaven, but the Light of the world” — of mankind who “sit in darkness, and in the shadow of death.” Chrysostom hears him answering the slur about Galilee (John 7:52): not a mere prophet of Galilee or Judea, but “the Lord of the whole earth.” Augustine turns it to exhortation: “let us follow Christ, the light of the world, that we may not be walking in darkness” — not the darkness “of the eyes, but that of the moral character.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, calls it “a beautiful commendation of Christ.” Since “we are all blind by nature, a remedy is offered, by which we may be… made partakers of the true light,” and the offer is universal, removing “the distinction, not only between Jews and Gentiles, but between the learned and ignorant.” Yet, he warns, men “will never present themselves to Christ to be illuminated” until they first own their blindness. Wesley adds the promise: he who “closely, humbly, steadily follows” shall have “the Divine light continually shining upon him… till he is guided by it to life everlasting.”
13:
“The Pharisees therefore said unto him, Thou bearest record of thyself; thy record is not true.” The witnesses explain the objection as a technical rule of evidence. The grammatical-historical commentators note the Jewish maxim that “no man can give witness for himself” (Robertson), and that the Law required two witnesses (Deuteronomy 17:6); since Jesus had only his own assertion, they held it “not true,” that is, “not pertinent,” “not worthy of belief” (Barnes). Ellicott catches the irony: standing “in the light of day, they demand a formal proof that the Sun has risen.” Lightfoot draws a vivid parallel from the rabbinic procedure for certifying the new moon — two known witnesses attesting before the Sanhedrin — which lights up Christ’s appeal to two witnesses, himself and the Father, in v.18. Robertson observes they were “still in the region of pedantic rules and external tests,” missing the substance for the form.

The fathers and the Reformers look past the technicality to the deeper claim. The Eastern Chrysostom says Christ “overthrew their argument”: “though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true” — an accommodation to those “who thought Him no more than a mere man” — and gives the reason, “for I know whence I come and whither I go,” that is, “I am God, from God, and the Son of God,” said obliquely after his “habit of mingling lofty and lowly words.” Alcuin answers their premise: far from witnessing alone, Christ “had, before His incarnation, sent many witnesses to prophesy of His Sacraments,” and asks why they reject “Mine and My Father’s testimony, the highest and most sure of all.” Calvin, for the Reformed, grants the common rule “that no man ought to be trusted when speaking in his own cause,” but the point is that Christ is no ordinary man pleading his own case. Wesley notes they “retort upon our Lord his own words” from John 5:31, to which he now replies that, being “inseparably united to the Father,” his case is unique.

The witnesses agree that the dispute exposes the Pharisees’ deepest error: pressing the letter of the law of evidence, they refuse the one testimony that could satisfy it, since the Father whom they demand as a second witness is the very God they claim to serve. They ask for two witnesses and will not hear either — neither the Son who speaks before them nor the Father who sent him.
14:
“Though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true: for I know whence I came, and whither I go; but ye cannot tell whence I come, and whither I go.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s claim that, in his case, the rule of two witnesses cannot apply. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the emphatic pronoun and the ground of the exception. Robertson: “even if” (kan) he bears witness of himself, “his own witness concerning himself is true… he can and does tell the truth all by himself concerning himself.” Ellicott explains why the canon fails here: “the requirement of two witnesses was based on the imperfection of individual knowledge, and the untrustworthiness of individual veracity,” but Christ’s evidence is “that of One who knew every circumstance” — “they claim a human proof of that which transcends human knowledge… a truth for which there could not possibly be a human witness.” Barnes adds that Jesus “knew by what authority he acted… by whom he was sent,” while they “could not determine this.”

The phrase “whence I came, and whither I go” the witnesses take as the whole compass of his person and mission. Clarke: “I came from God, and am going to God, and can neither do nor say any thing but what leads to and glorifies him.” Wesley sees in the two clauses “the two heads… to which all the doctrine concerning Christ may be referred” — the former treated in v.16, the latter from v.21 — and reads “whence I came” as “from God, both as God and as man.” The Pharisees’ blindness, all agree, lies just here: judging by the flesh, they cannot trace his origin or his end, and so cannot weigh the testimony of one whose witness rests on a self-knowledge they do not share. Their demand for a second witness is answered by the claim that the witness they doubt is the one being in the world able to speak the whole truth about himself.

The witnesses note, finally, that the verse quietly reverses the charge. The Pharisees had said his witness was “not true”; Christ replies that it is precisely true because it rests on a knowledge no one else possesses. Where they see only a Galilean of known parentage, he sees, and speaks from, his eternal origin and destiny — so that the very self-knowledge they cannot share is the ground on which his solitary testimony stands unimpeachable.
15:
“Ye judge after the flesh; I judge no man.” The witnesses set the two clauses in deliberate contrast. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “after the flesh” (kata sarka) as judging “according to appearance” and “carnal… prejudices” (Barnes, Gill): the Pharisees “looked at the form of human flesh, and declared His witness false” (Ellicott), judging by the standards of the flesh (2 Corinthians 5:16) rather than by the spiritual meaning of his words. Robertson sets it against the chapter’s theme: “the Light of the World had come, but they loved darkness rather than light” (John 3:19), blinded by “the god of this age” (2 Corinthians 4:4). Clarke hears Christ speaking “exactly in the character of an ambassador,” whose credentials, not a second voucher, prove his commission.

On “I judge no man” the commentators offer complementary senses. Barnes and Wesley take it of manner and time: Jesus “judged no one after their manner,” not coming “to censure and condemn men after the appearance” (John 3:17), and so “not thus, not now, not at my first coming” (Wesley). Calvin weighs the readings — whether the contrast is that they judge carnally while he does not, or that he refrains for now from the office of judge — and inclines to the point that they are “not lawful and competent judges, because they have not the Spirit for their guide,” while Christ’s judgment, when he gives it, is never “after the flesh.” The Latin Augustine, prefacing his homily, praises John as the eagle who “soared… above the whole army of angels” to the Word himself — a reminder that the One the Pharisees judge so superficially is the very Light their flesh-bound eyes cannot see. The verse turns their verdict back on them: the judges of the Light are themselves judged by the darkness in which they stand.

Gill notes a second possible rendering — “ye that are after the flesh judge,” that is, carnal men passing carnal verdicts on spiritual things — which only sharpens the contrast. The witnesses draw the lesson that judgment formed by appearances and prejudice can never reach the truth of Christ; the Pharisees, confident in their verdict, are in fact disqualified as judges by the very flesh-bound vision they trust, while the One they dismiss withholds, for now, the judgment that is his to give.
16:
“And yet if I judge, my judgment is true: for I am not alone, but I and the Father that sent me.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s correction, lest his “I judge no man” seem to surrender his right. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the quality of the judgment. Robertson notes the word is not merely “true” (alēthes) in the particular facts of v.14 but “true” (alēthinē) in the deeper sense of “genuine, soundly based… satisfying our perfect conception” (Westcott; cf. John 1:9). Gill grounds it in Christ’s omniscience: he “looked into the heart… to whom all things are naked and open, and therefore cannot be deceived.” The reason given — “I am not alone, but I and the Father” — Barnes explains as the union that guarantees the verdict: “I concur with the Father who hath sent me… my judgment would coincide with all that God had purposed.” Ellicott adds that, though “judgment was not the object of His mission,” it was “the result of the manifestation of the Light” (John 3:19), and is here identified “with the eternal and unchangeable truth of the Father.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, asks why Christ “does not rather make an open assertion of his Divinity,” and answers that “as his Divinity was concealed under the veil of the flesh, he brings forward his Father, in whom it was more manifest,” so that all he does and teaches rests on the Father’s authority. Wesley presses the unity further: “his Father is in him, and he is in the Father” (John 14:10), “not one and another God, but one God, though distinct persons, and so inseparable.” The single judge is thus never solitary; his sentence carries the weight of the Father with whom he is one, and so cannot but be true.

The witnesses find in the verse an early glimpse of the doctrine the whole Gospel unfolds: that the Son does nothing in isolation from the Father, and that his every word and act carries the Father’s authority. His judgment is true not merely because it is well-informed but because it is the joint act of the Son and the Father who are one — so that to weigh his verdict is, whether the Pharisees know it or not, to weigh the judgment of God himself.
17:
“It is also written in your law, that the testimony of two men is true.” The witnesses read this as Christ meeting the Pharisees on their own ground. The grammatical-historical commentators note the pointed phrase “your law,” addressed “to those who were professed expounders of it and accused Him of being a transgressor of it” (Ellicott); Robertson observes the same idiom at John 10:34, and cautions that it does not mean Christ “separates himself wholly from the Jews and the law,” for he upholds it (Matthew 5:17). The rule invoked is the Mosaic requirement of two or three witnesses in a grave case (Deuteronomy 17:6; Deuteronomy 19:15; cf. Matthew 18:16), which Barnes presses to its conclusion: if two human witnesses establish a fact, “the evidence of Jesus and of God ought not to be deemed insufficient.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, anticipates the obvious objection — that no man is received as a witness in his own cause — and answers as before: “the Son of God ought to be excluded from the ordinary number of other men,” for “he neither is a private individual, nor transacts his own private business.” His distinguishing of himself from the Father, Calvin adds, is an accommodation “to the capacity of his hearers… because he was at that time a servant of the Father, from whom all his doctrine has proceeded.” Henry, for the Puritans, draws the devotional turn: “if we knew Christ better, we should know the Father better,” for “those who know not his glory and grace, know not the Father that sent him.” The argument thus stands: by the Jews’ own law two concurring witnesses settle a matter, and Christ offers two — himself and the Father.

Robertson adds a careful qualification: the combined witness of two “is not true just because they agree, unless true in fact separately” — the Law’s rule assumes each witness is independently honest, which is exactly the case here. Henry, looking ahead in the discourse, draws the believer’s comfort that runs beneath the controversy: “the time of our departure out of the world depends upon God,” so that “our enemies cannot hasten it… nor can our friends delay it,” and every true believer “can look up and say with pleasure, My times are in thy hand, and better there than in my own.” The two-witness argument thus does double duty, answering the Pharisees on their own terms while pointing past them to the Father whose testimony and timing govern all.
18:
“I am one that bear witness of myself, and the Father that sent me beareth witness of me.” The witnesses take this as the formal answer to the charge of single testimony. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic construction — “I am He who beareth witness” (Ellicott), there being “no word to express the English ‘one’” — and identify the two witnesses Robertson says “Jesus presents to the Pharisees in defence of his claim to be the Light of the World” (v.12). Barnes weighs why such self-witness may here be admitted: “in human courts a man is not allowed to bear witness of himself, because he has a personal interest,” but where “one has no party ends to serve; when he is willing to deny himself… his own testimony may be admitted in evidence of his motives and designs,” as with Jesus and his apostles.

Ellicott unfolds the twofold witness: first, Christ’s own individuality — “I, who know whence I came and whither I go, testify of Myself to you who know neither”; and second, “the fact that the Father sent Him,” so that “He is the Ambassador from the Father, accredited by the Father’s works and the voice from heaven,” and “His word is official as well as personal.” The thought, he notes, is bound closely to v.16 and to the close of John 5: as his judgment is not individual judgment because of the union with the Father, so his witness is not individual witness but the Father’s also. The two required witnesses are thus the Son and the Father — the very pair the Pharisees, demanding proof, are least willing to receive.

The witnesses recall that the Father’s witness is no bare assertion but had been spelled out before: the works the Father gave Jesus to do, the voice from heaven, and the Scriptures and Moses that testify of him (compare John 5:36–39). So the second witness the Law demands is not absent but everywhere — in heaven’s voice, in the signs, in the prophets — and the Pharisees’ blindness is not for lack of evidence but for unwillingness to see. The deepest irony, the commentators agree, is that the very pair of witnesses they require, Father and Son, stand before them in the one person they reject; demanding two, they will not receive either, and so condemn themselves by their own rule of evidence.
19:
“Then said they unto him, Where is thy Father?” The witnesses agree the question was a sneer, and that it proved the very fault Christ had named. The Latin and Eastern fathers read it so: Augustine says those who had heard “ye judge after the flesh” at once “showed that they did so,” understanding his Father “in a carnal sense” — “we see you alone; prove to us then that Your Father is with You.” Theophylact hears it spoken “in contumely and contempt,” insinuating either that he was “born of fornication, and knows not who His Father is,” or sneering at the “low situation of His father,” Joseph the obscure. The grammatical-historical commentators concur: Gill paraphrases the taunt — “it is Joseph the carpenter you mean; a goodly witness indeed!” — and Barnes notes it was asked “in derision,” the Jews professing ignorance of what Jesus had often meant by “my Father” (John 5:17).

Christ’s reply — “Ye neither know me, nor my Father: if ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also” — the witnesses take as both rebuke and revelation. He answers, Augustine notes, those who asked “to tempt Him, not to get at the truth.” The saying makes the knowledge of the Father wholly dependent on the knowledge of the Son: to know Christ is to know the Father, and to be ignorant of Christ is to be ignorant of God, however loudly one claims him. Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that the Jews’ boasted knowledge of God was empty, since “God is not known but in the face of Christ”; their question “Where is thy Father?” thus betrays a blindness not merely to Jesus’ parentage but to the God they professed to serve. Henry adds the comfort underneath the rebuke: those who would know God truly must “learn of Christ,” for apart from him they “become vain in their imaginations concerning God.”

Robertson and Clarke note that the Jews “professed ignorance” of what Jesus meant by “my Father,” though he had made it plain repeatedly; their question is thus less a request for information than a refusal of light. Ellicott observes that the saying gathers the chapter’s argument to a point: the Father is known only through the Son, so the demand to “see” the Father apart from Christ is self-defeating. The witnesses leave the verse on its solemn double edge — a rebuke to all who claim to know God while rejecting his Son, and a promise that whoever truly knows Christ has, in him, found the Father also.
20:
“These words spake Jesus in the treasury, as he taught in the temple: and no man laid hands on him; for his hour was not yet come.” The witnesses fasten on the place and on the divine restraint. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the treasury (gazophylakion) as the area in the Court of the Women where stood the thirteen trumpet-shaped chests for the worshippers’ offerings (Gill, Lightfoot, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson) — “one of the most public places in the Temple area” (Ellicott), close by the very chamber where the Sanhedrin sat. Robertson notes Jesus “hardly taught within a treasure-chamber,” so the phrase means “at the treasury,” and reads the “and” as “and yet”: he taught openly, in the teacher’s chair, the very things that stung them, and yet “no man laid hands on him.”

The Eastern Chrysostom marvels at the Jews’ folly: having sought him before the feast, then found him “in the midst of them,” and often “attempted to take Him… without being able,” they “were not even so awed by His power, but set themselves to their wickedness, and desisted not.” He notes that Jesus spoke “in the character of teacher, which was more adapted to rouse them,” pressing the very claims of equality with the Father that enraged them. Barnes and Calvin draw the doctrine of providence. Barnes: the restraint “proves that God has power over wicked men to control them, and to make them accomplish his own purposes.” Calvin, for the Reformed, marvels that men who “held an undisputed sway” in the temple, and “might have banished Christ… by a single word,” were instead “constrained to endure Christ openly” — an “astonishing power of God,” who “caused men to hear him, and guarded him.” The clause “his hour was not yet come” (compare John 7:30), Augustine will insist, frees Christ from any “fatal necessity”: he dies not when his enemies will, but when he himself appoints.

Ellicott adds the telling detail that the chamber where the Sanhedrin held its sessions stood close by this very court, so that Christ taught “openly and fearlessly” within earshot of the council plotting his death. The witnesses thus read the verse as a quiet display of sovereignty: the One whose hour the Father alone appoints stands unguarded among armed enemies, and they cannot move a hand against him. The lesson, Calvin notes, is that no servant of God is in his enemies’ power a moment before God permits.
21:
“Then said Jesus again unto them, I go my way, and ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins: whither I go, ye cannot come.” The witnesses hear in this a turn from grace to warning. Henry, for the Puritans, marks the change of tone: Christ “had piped to them in the offers of his grace, and they had not danced; now he mourns to them in the denunciations of his wrath,” yet still speaks “in kindness to those few who received his doctrine.” The grammatical-historical commentators expound the threefold sentence. “I go my way” (compare John 7:33) points to his death and return to the Father; “ye shall seek me” is, Robertson says, “the search of despair,” seeking the Messiah “when it is too late, the tragedy of Judaism” (John 1:11); and “ye shall die in your sin” (singular here, plural in v.24 — “sin in its essence, sin in its acts”) means, Barnes warns, to “die unpardoned,” since “there is no way of pardon but by him” (Acts 4:12).

The fathers find in “I go my way” the freedom of Christ’s death. Augustine reads it of “His passion, as a free, and not a compulsory sacrifice,” death being for him “a return to the place whence He had come.” Theophylact hears the promise that “He will rise again in glory, and sit at the right hand of God.” Origen presses the paradox that even his persecutors will “seek” him, though to seek Jesus is properly “to seek truth and wisdom,” and notes that the saying shows “He had power over His own death.” Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the warning: the Gospel “is not thrown uselessly into the air, but must breathe” life or death (2 Corinthians 2:16); “the wicked will at length feel how great loss they have suffered by rejecting Christ… but it will be too late, for there will be no more room for repentance.” The verse thus sets before the unbelieving a closing door: the One they spurn is going where their unrepented sin can never follow.
22:
“Then said the Jews, Will he kill himself? because he saith, Whither I go, ye cannot come.” The witnesses read the question as bitter, ignorant mockery. The grammatical-historical commentators note the grammar of contempt: the form expects the answer “no,” Robertson says, but carries “a manifest sneer,” “alike subtle and bitter” (Vincent). They had earlier guessed, in scorn, that he meant to go to the Greek Dispersion (John 7:35); now, Clarke notes, “they understood that he spoke concerning his death,” and Ellicott explains their grim logic: “there is only one class of persons who can speak with certainty of thus going away — those who by their own act fix the limit of their own lives.” Robertson adds the cruelest edge: since the rabbis held that suicides went to the deepest Gehenna (so Josephus), the taunt suggests that “of course the rabbis could not join Jesus there.”

Gill marks the malice and folly together: the inference “by no means followed,” and in suggesting it they “imitated Satan,” hoping he might indeed do it and so “extricate them out of their difficulties.” Barnes weighs whether the question sprang “from ignorance or malice,” and reads in it “mingled hatred and contempt” — “he is a deceiver… he is mad, and it is probable he will go on and kill himself.” Yet, Barnes adds, the scene displays “the wonderful patience of Jesus in enduring the contradiction of sinners,” who “bore contempt without rendering railing for railing.” Calvin, for the Reformed, sees the same “fearless scorn” and “effrontery”: they reckoned Christ’s absence “a matter of no moment,” supposing they would “gain a victory over him.” The mockery, the witnesses agree, is darker than the mockers know: groping for an insult, they stumble near the truth — that his death will indeed be of his own laying down, and that whither he goes, unbelief can never come.

The witnesses note how the chapter’s tragic irony deepens here. Twice now the Jews have wrenched a literal, contemptuous sense from words heavy with saving truth — first imagining a journey to the Greeks (John 7:35), now suicide — and each time their mockery brushes against a reality they cannot grasp: that he goes freely to a death they cannot share, and to a Father they do not know. Their scorn, the commentators agree, is itself the judgment Christ has just pronounced, the darkened mind of those who will “die in their sins.”
23:
“And he said unto them, Ye are from beneath; I am from above: ye are of this world; I am not of this world.” The witnesses read this as a sweeping contrast of origin and nature. The grammatical-historical commentators take “from beneath” and “of this world” as parallel terms for the earthly and corrupt: “you are of the earth,” Barnes says, “influenced by earthly, sensual, and corrupt passions,” while Christ’s “views are heavenly.” Robertson agrees the antithesis of katō and anō is “between the earthly (sensual) and the heavenly,” as in James 3:15–17 and Colossians 3:1 — “these proud rabbis had their origin in this world of darkness,” though he notes the language could glance at the saying that they came from Gehenna as “children of the devil” (v.44). Ellicott guards the sense: the gulf is “not that between souls in Abraham’s bosom and souls in Hades,” but “between heaven and earth,” the two pairs of clauses interpreting each other. Clarke reads it sharply against their taunt: “ye are capable of murder, and of self-murder too, because ye have nothing of God in you.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds in it the root of their unbelief. They “do not receive his doctrine, because they have an utter dislike of the kingdom of God”; under “of this world” he “includes all that men naturally possess,” pointing to “the disagreement which exists between his Gospel and the ingenuity… of the human mind,” for “the Gospel is heavenly wisdom, but our mind grovels on the earth.” Hence, he concludes, “no man will ever be qualified to become a disciple of Christ, till Christ has formed him by his Spirit,” and so “faith is so seldom found in the world.” Wesley simply notes that Christ here “directly shows whence he came, even from heaven, and whither he goes.” The verse thus diagnoses the Jews’ blindness as a matter not of intellect but of origin: earth-born minds cannot, of themselves, receive the wisdom of heaven.

The witnesses draw the pastoral warning. If the difference between Christ and his hearers is one of origin — heaven against earth — then no mere argument or evidence will bridge it; what is required is a new birth from above. The verse thus presses every reader to ask not merely whether he understands Christ’s words but whether he has been born of the Spirit who alone makes the heavenly wisdom welcome, lest, like the rabbis, he prove earth-bound in the presence of the Light himself.
24:
“I said therefore unto you, that ye shall die in your sins: for if ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins.” The witnesses note the change from the singular “sin” of v.21 to the plural “sins,” and the great claim folded into “I am he.” On the first, Ellicott observes that the singular pointed to “the believing not” as “itself a state of sin” (John 16:9), while the plural “brings home to them the definite and known acts of sin which resulted from their sinful state.” Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the same point: unbelief “is the source and cause of all evils,” not because it is the only sin, but “because it drives us away from Christ, and deprives us of his grace, from which we ought to expect deliverance from all our sins”; that the Jews “reject the medicine with obstinate malice… is their mortal disease.”

On “that I am he” the commentators hear the chapter’s deepest note. Robertson lists the possibilities — “that I am from above” (v.23), the Messiah, the Light of the World, the Deliverer from sin — but adds that the words may be taken “in the absolute sense as the Jews used the language of Jehovah,” the great “I AM” of Deuteronomy 32:39 and Isaiah 43:10. Wesley takes it so outright: “here our Lord claims the Divine name, I AM” (Exodus 3:14), as plainly as in v.58. Barnes keeps to “the Messiah,” the title Jesus had professed. Either way the witnesses agree that the verse fastens salvation to the person of Christ: to refuse to believe who he is, is to die unforgiven, for there is no pardon apart from him.

The witnesses also note the structure of the warning: the sentence is framed as a condition with one escape. The Jews are not told they must die in their sins, but that they will do so unless they believe — so that the very threat is an offer, the closing door still ajar. Christ presses the whole weight of salvation upon faith in who he is, leaving them, even in this stern word, the one path by which the sentence may yet be reversed.
25:
“Then said they unto him, Who art thou?” The witnesses agree the question is contemptuous — “Thou, who art thou?” (Robertson, Ellicott), a phrase “in frequent use to express contempt” — and that they take his “I am” as an unfinished sentence rather than the divine Name, which by then was “too sacred to be uttered” (Ellicott). Barnes admires the patience with which Jesus, “a mere pretender from Galilee” in their eyes, “poor and without friends,” bore the scorn and “answered them.”

Christ’s reply — rendered in the Authorised Version “Even the same that I said unto you from the beginning” — is famously hard, and the witnesses divide over it. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot favours the temporal sense: “the same that I told you from the beginning,” pointing back to his open profession before the Sanhedrin in John 5. Clarke notes “a variety of renderings,” one taking tēn archēn as “the chief, the supreme” — “I am the Beginning” — and Robertson grants the sentence may be affirmation, question, or exclamation, while insisting the adverbial accusative “cannot mean ‘from the beginning’” in the simple sense. The Latin Augustine reads it doctrinally: “Believe Me to be the beginning” (the Greek archē being feminine), for “the beginning cannot be changed; it remains ever as it is.” Calvin, for the Reformed, rejects the rendering “I am the beginning” as a claim of eternal Divinity — “there is no ambiguity of this sort in the Greek” — and notes that even the Greek fathers “differ as to the meaning,” some giving tēn archēn the force of “altogether” or “this ought to be observed.” Wesley keeps to the plainest sense: “the same which I say to you, as it were in one discourse, with one even tenor from the time I first spake.” Beneath the textual tangle the witnesses find one clear point: Christ refers them back to all he has already claimed — Light of the world, sent of the Father — and bids them believe it.

The witnesses agree, beneath the grammatical tangle, that the question itself is telling. Having heard him claim to be the Light of the world, the bread from heaven, the one sent of the Father, they still ask “Who art thou?” — not from ignorance of what he had said, but from refusal to receive it. His answer, whatever its precise rendering, sends them back to his own consistent testimony; he will not frame a new claim for mockers, but holds them to the words they have already despised.
26:
“I have many things to say and to judge of you: but he that sent me is true; and I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him.” The witnesses read the verse as Christ restraining his rightful judgment and grounding his message in the Father. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the first clause: being omniscient, Gill says, Christ “knew their persons and actions… and all their sins,” which he “could justly have… charged them with,” but “this was not his present business, he came not to judge and condemn, but to save,” leaving their reckoning to the Father. Lightfoot reads it differently and richly: “he that sent me hath of old said and judged of you, and he is true” — the Father having already judged them in prophecy (Isaiah 6:10; Isaiah 29:10), from which Christ drew the sentence “ye shall die in your sins.” Barnes simply notes the warning beneath the restraint: “there are many things in you which I might condemn,” but “he that sent me is true,” and his declarations about men “are to be credited.”

The second half the witnesses take as the key to all Christ’s teaching: “I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him.” His message is not his own invention but the Father’s, delivered faithfully — the same claim made at John 7:16 and v.16. The Latin Augustine, pondering Christ’s having called himself “the Beginning,” asks whether the Father too is the Beginning, and answers within the unity of the Godhead. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the solemnity: Christ “expressly summons them to the judgment-seat of God,” the truthfulness of the Father guaranteeing that the warning will not fail. The verse thus binds Christ’s word doubly to the Father — as the One who has already judged Israel truly, and as the source from whom Jesus speaks only what he has heard.

The witnesses observe how the verse holds judgment and mercy together. Christ has “many things” he could justly say against them, and the truthful Father has already condemned their unbelief in the prophets; yet his present errand to the world is not condemnation but the faithful delivery of what he has heard. The restraint is itself grace — the Judge who could speak their sentence chooses instead to speak the Father’s saving word, leaving the fuller judgment to a day still to come.
27:
“They understood not that he spake to them of the Father.” The witnesses treat John’s aside as a mark of the Jews’ deep blindness. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh whether it was ignorance or unwillingness: Barnes thinks “they doubtless understood that he meant to speak of God, but they were unwilling to acknowledge that he really came from God,” while Robertson, with Westcott, blames their being “preoccupied… with thoughts of an earthly deliverer” and “prejudiced against recognizing Jesus as the one sent from God.” Ellicott finds the failure genuinely astonishing — “the statement of their want of perception… is made just because it was marvellous” — and pinpoints it: “they did not know that the Sender and the Father are one.” Gill ascribes it to a judicial hardening: “their hearts were made fat, and hardened, and their eyes were blinded.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the sober application. “Hence we see how stupid those men are whose understandings are possessed by Satan”; nothing could be plainer than that “they were summoned to the judgment-seat of God,” yet “they are altogether blind.” This, he warns, “happens daily to other enemies of the Gospel,” and “such blindness ought to instruct us to walk with fear.” The witnesses thus read the verse as more than a note of fact: it is a window on the mystery of unbelief, in which men hear the plainest words about God and the Father and yet, for the hardness of their hearts, perceive nothing — a warning to every later hearer not to presume upon mere understanding without the softening grace of the Spirit.

The witnesses note the dramatic irony John intends. The evangelist, writing long after, records the failure precisely because it was so astonishing: men who prided themselves on knowing God could not perceive that the One speaking of his Sender was speaking of the Father, and of himself as one with him. The verse thus prepares the next, where Christ promises that only the lifting up of the Son of man will at last force upon them the recognition they now refuse.

Wesley adds that this very failure is why, in the verses that follow (vv.28–29), Christ “speaks plainly of the Father, and again claims the Divine name, I AM” — patiently meeting their dullness with a clearer disclosure. The witnesses thus see the verse not as the end of the dialogue but as its hinge: their blindness draws from Christ a still plainer word, so that the failure of perception becomes the occasion of a fuller revelation.
28:
“When ye have lifted up the Son of man, then shall ye know that I am he, and that I do nothing of myself; but as my Father hath taught me, I speak these things.” The witnesses read “lifted up” of the cross — and beyond it. The grammatical-historical commentators note the word hypsoō, used in John of the crucifixion (John 3:14; John 12:32), and Ellicott adds that “both the Crucifixion and Ascension are implied,” the Jews now “marked out as the instruments of the Crucifixion” (Acts 3:15) and so the means of his return to the Father’s throne. Clarke notes that “to be lifted up” was a Jewish idiom “for to die.” The promised knowledge, Barnes says, would come “by the miracles that attended the death of Jesus… but chiefly by his resurrection from the dead”; yet Robertson warns it would be knowledge that “will come too late and will not bring a change of heart,” the Spirit convicting them “concerning judgment” (John 16:8).

The Latin Augustine hears in “that I am he” the divine Name: “Recollect the words, I am that I am” (Exodus 3:14), “and you will know why I say, I am.” The lifting up “means the lifting up of the cross,” “accomplished by the hands of those who should afterwards believe” — so that even his crucifiers might be saved. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the severity: offended at their stupidity, Christ “declares that they do not deserve that he should open his mouth” further, yet foretells “the time will yet come, when you shall know that a Prophet of God has lived among you” — a knowledge that comes “too late,” when the reprobate are “dragged to punishment.” The verse joins Christ’s self-disclosure to his obedience: lifted up, he will be known as the great “I am,” who does “nothing of himself” but speaks only as the Father taught him.

The witnesses gather the verse’s two claims into one. The cross, which the Jews will raise in malice, becomes the throne from which the truth of Christ is proved; and the One there lifted up is shown to be both the eternal “I am” and the perfectly obedient Son, who originates nothing of himself but speaks only as the Father taught him. So the deepest assertion of his Deity and the fullest confession of his dependence stand together — the divine Name borne by the Son who does always the Father’s will.
29:
“And he that sent me is with me: the Father hath not left me alone; for I do always those things that please him.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s confidence in the Father’s abiding presence. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the unbroken fellowship: though “men had forsaken and rejected him, yet God attended him” (Barnes); Robertson notes that “however much the crowds and the disciples misunderstood or left Jesus, the Father always comforted and understood him” (cf. Matthew 14:23). Ellicott applies the words “to every moment of life,” a “Presence which they knew not of,” to which Christ would cling even in the hour when “the disciples shall be scattered… and He shall be left as men would think alone” (John 16:32). Clarke and Barnes ground the unbroken favour in Christ’s perfect obedience — “I delight to do thy will, O my God” (Psalm 40:8) — the Father’s “well pleased” (Matthew 3:17) resting on the Son who “always” pleases him.

Calvin, for the Reformed, widens the comfort to every faithful minister. Christ “boasts that God, under whose guidance… he does every thing, will assist him, so that he shall not labor in vain”; and “all faithful teachers ought to be endued with the same confidence,” knowing that “the hand of God will be near them” when “with a pure conscience they discharge such a ministry as he demands,” for God “makes his word successful by the secret efficacy of his Spirit.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both Christology and consolation: the Son is never alone because he never ceases to please the Father, and those who follow him in obedience share, in their measure, the same sustaining presence.

The witnesses note how this verse answers the loneliness implied in the Jews’ rejection. Surrounded by enemies and soon to be abandoned by his own, Christ is yet never solitary, for the Father’s presence rests upon his unbroken obedience. The ground of that presence — “I do always those things that please him” — is held out as the secret of all communion with God: the soul that delights to do God’s will is never left alone, but walks, as the Son did, in the company of the Father.
30:
“As he spake these words, many believed on him.” The witnesses pause on this surprising harvest amid hostility. The grammatical-historical commentators marvel at it as much as at the unbelief of v.27: Ellicott notes that “wonder has often been expressed at the want of apprehension” just recorded, and finds “no less room for wonder in the faith spoken of here.” Gill attributes the change to the manner and matter of Christ’s words — “spoken… with majesty and authority,” coming “with power” — and Barnes to “the convincing nature and force of the truths” he had set forth, by which “many… were made penitent and believing.” Both Barnes and Clarke quote the proverb that captures the double effect of the one discourse: “the same sun that hardens the clay softens the wax.”

The same word, the witnesses observe, had been “the savor of death unto death to the obstinate Pharisees” and “the savor of life unto life to many of the simple-hearted people” (Clarke; cf. 2 Corinthians 2:16). Yet the commentators note, looking ahead, that this faith would soon be tested: the very verses following (vv.31–32) address “those Jews which believed on him,” and the bitter dispute that ensues will show how shallow some of that believing was. For now, the verse stands as a quiet testimony that even in the hour of fiercest contradiction, Christ’s word was not spoken in vain, but drew a remnant out of the hardened crowd into the beginnings of faith.

The witnesses close the section on a note of hope tempered by realism. That “many believed” shows the word of Christ is never wholly without fruit, even where it provokes the fiercest hatred; the same truth that condemns the hardened saves the willing. Yet the commentators are careful not to overstate this faith, for the dialogue that follows will sift it severely, distinguishing a belief that abides in his word from one that withers under testing — a reminder that to begin to believe is not yet to be a disciple indeed.

Henry and the others find encouragement here for every preacher of the gospel: where the truth seems only to harden, it is at the same time secretly softening hearts unseen, and the labour that meets contradiction is never therefore fruitless. The verse stands as a quiet pledge that the word of Christ, faithfully spoken, will always gather its remnant, even out of a crowd bent on his death.
31:
“Then said Jesus to those Jews which believed on him, If ye continue in my word, then are ye my disciples indeed.” The witnesses read this as Christ testing a new and shallow faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note the precise phrasing: Robertson observes they “believed him” (dative autō, his claims) rather than “believed on him” (eis auton) as in v.30, so that they “did not commit themselves to him.” The condition “if ye abide in my word” (a constative aorist) makes “future loyalty… the test of the reality of their present profession.” Ellicott catches the note of wonder in the phrase “Jews and yet believers,” and links the “abiding” to John 15:7. Barnes presses the pastoral caution: their faith, born “under a single sermon,” had “had no time to test,” and only if it “should produce obedience… and a holy life” would it prove genuine, “for the tree is known by its fruit” (1 John 2:4). Henry, for the Puritans, hears Christ turning “from the proud Pharisees” to “those few feeble Jews that believed,” speaking comfort “to those that tremble at his word.”

The Eastern Chrysostom finds in “if ye continue” a searching of hearts: it “made it manifest what was in their hearts,” a “tacit rebuke to some who had believed and afterwards withdrawn.” He grounds endurance in deep-rooted doctrine, as the prophet prayed, “Nail my flesh by thy fear” (Psalm 119:120) — “the soul nailed by the fear of God none will be able to overturn.” Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the same lesson of perseverance: “it is not enough for any one to have begun well, if their progress to the end do not correspond,” and Christ “distinguishes his followers from hypocrites by this mark,” that “believers persevere constantly to the end.” The witnesses thus agree that this verse, addressed to fresh believers, lays down the abiding test of true discipleship — not the first stirring of faith but its perseverance in Christ’s word.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, draws the humbling corollary: “we have all one Master, and are fellow disciples under Him,” for “He is the Master of all, who dwells in the hearts of all.” To continue in his word is thus not to graduate beyond the need of teaching but to remain, lifelong, a learner at his feet — the very posture the proud Pharisees would not take, and the test these new believers were about to fail.
32:
“And ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free.” The witnesses unfold both the knowledge promised and the freedom it brings. The grammatical-historical commentators take “the truth” as the gospel, and ultimately Christ himself: Robertson notes truth is “one of the marks of Christ” (John 1:14), who will claim to Thomas to be its personification (John 14:6), and that it is a knowledge “learned by doing God’s will” (John 7:17). Ellicott presses that “these Jews professed to know the truth… but they had yet to learn that truth was not only a system, but also a power; not only something to be written or spoken, but also something to be felt and lived.” On the freedom, the commentators agree it is freedom from sin’s slavery, not political: Barnes calls it deliverance “from the slavery of evil passions, corrupt propensities” (Romans 6:16), and Robertson links it to Paul’s great word for liberty (Galatians 5:1; Romans 8:2). Clarke recalls the rabbinic maxim that “no man was free but he who exercised himself in the meditation of the law,” and answers it: “no man is truly free, but he in whose heart the power of sin is destroyed,” who has received “the Spirit of adoption” crying “Abba, Father” (Romans 8:15).

Calvin, for the Reformed, dwells on the growth in knowledge promised to perseverance. Though those Christ addressed “were as yet uneducated, and scarcely knew the first elements,” he promises “a more full understanding of his doctrine”; and the statement is general, so that “whatever progress any of us have made in the Gospel, let him know that he needs new additions.” This deeper knowledge is itself Christ’s gift, “for it is he who impresses his word on our hearts by his Spirit, and… daily chases away the clouds” of ignorance. The witnesses thus join knowing and being freed: the truth that liberates is not a doctrine merely grasped but a Person known, whose Spirit breaks the bondage of sin in the heart that abides in his word.
33:
“They answered him, We be Abraham’s seed, and were never in bondage to any man: how sayest thou, Ye shall be made free?” Two questions occupy the witnesses: who speaks, and how their boast could be made. On the speakers, the commentators divide. Gill, Clarke, and Wesley take them to be the unbelieving Pharisees standing by, since the murderous words that follow (v.40, 44) seem impossible from believers; Ellicott and Robertson, with Calvin, hold rather that these are “the Jews who had believed” (v.31), whose faith, tested, “fails in the first steps of discipleship” — the explanation lying in Christ’s own warning about continuing. On the boast, all agree it “passes the limits of historical truth” (Ellicott). Lightfoot documents the inordinate Jewish pride in Abrahamic descent; Robertson calls “we be Abraham’s seed” “the proudest boast of the Jews,” but “Were never in bondage” “a palpable untruth uttered in the heat of controversy,” when “at that very moment the Jews wore the Roman yoke,” as they had worn that of Egypt, Babylon, Persia, and the rest. Clarke calls the claim “ridiculous in the extreme,” their whole history “full of recitals of their servitude.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the boast charitably as a confused cry “by despisers rather than by those who believed,” and explains the form: Scripture often ascribes “to all what belongs only to a part.” He notes too that they leaned on their election and adoption (Romans 9:4), unable to bear being told that a holy and chosen people were in any bondage. The witnesses thus find the answer self-condemning: misunderstanding Christ’s offer of spiritual freedom as a slur on their national liberty, they betray, in a manifest falsehood, the very bondage of pride and unbelief from which he came to free them.

The witnesses note the deep irony the boast exposes. The men who claimed they “were never in bondage” were at that moment bondsmen to Rome and, far more, slaves of the sin Christ was about to name; their indignant denial of all servitude is itself the proof of how blind they were to their truest chains. Pleading the freedom of Abraham’s seed, they could not see that descent from Abraham, without faith, had left them in a bondage no genealogy could break.
34:
“Verily, verily, I say unto you, Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin.” The witnesses take this as Christ correcting their misunderstanding by lifting the question from political to moral bondage. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that he “did not refer to political bondage, but to the slavery of the soul to evil passions and desires” (Barnes), the sinner being “bound to it as a slave is to his master” (Romans 6:16; 2 Peter 2:19). Clarke fixes the sense: this is “the slavery of which Christ spoke, and deliverance from it the liberty which he promised.” Ellicott presses the grammar — the present participle “committeth sin” expresses “the continuance of the deeds of sin,” not a single act — and notes that though the Cambridge manuscript omits “of sin,” the words “must be regarded as an integral part of the text.” Gill marks that Christ, “taking no notice of their civil liberty, to which he could easily have replied to their confusion,” instead presses home “their moral servitude and bondage.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the doctrine of the will’s bondage: the man who serves sin is no free agent but a slave, however he boasts of liberty, and only Christ can break the chain. Wesley adds that the bondage is universal until grace intervenes, and Cyril of Jerusalem turns the saying toward the deliverance of baptismal grace. The witnesses thus see the verse exposing the deepest irony of the Jews’ boast: priding themselves that they “were never in bondage,” they were, every one, the slaves of the sin that ruled them — and the freedom Christ offered was precisely the freedom from this servitude that no descent from Abraham could supply. The Son alone, the next verses will say, can make a slave a free son of the house.

The witnesses press the universality of the diagnosis. “Whosoever” admits no exception: Jew and Gentile, free citizen and slave, the man who keeps on sinning is sin’s bondsman, whatever his birth or station. This levels every proud distinction the Jews had raised, and prepares the gospel offer — that since all alike are slaves, all alike need the one Liberator, and the only freedom worth the name is the freedom from sin that the Son bestows.
35:
“And the servant abideth not in the house for ever: but the Son abideth ever.” The witnesses read this as Christ pressing the metaphor of slavery toward the gospel of sonship. The grammatical-historical commentators hear an allusion to Abraham’s own household: the slave Hagar and her son Ishmael were cast out, while Isaac, the son, remained the heir (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott; cf. Genesis 21:10; Galatians 4:30). The point, they explain, is that a slave “has no permanent standing in the family”; his place is precarious and may be ended at any time, whereas “the Son abideth ever.” So the Jews, slaves of sin, have no abiding place in God’s house by mere natural descent; only sonship secures a permanent inheritance. Robertson and Ellicott note the deliberate ambiguity of “the Son” — the figure of any true son in a household, but pointing beyond to the Son who abides eternally and alone can confer the freedom of sons.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the warning and the comfort together. The bondservant’s insecure tenure is the lot of all who remain in sin; they may dwell for a time in the visible church, as Ishmael in Abraham’s tent, but “shall not abide for ever.” Yet the Son’s eternal abiding is the ground of the believer’s hope, for those whom he makes free and adopts share his permanence in the Father’s house. The witnesses thus find in the verse a quiet but searching threat to the presumptuous — that physical kinship with Abraham, like a slave’s place in the house, gives no lasting title — and the promise that only union with the abiding Son makes one a son who abides.

The witnesses add that the figure quietly answers the Jews’ confidence in mere membership of the covenant people. To be in the house as a servant is not to belong to it as a son; and a servant may be put out, as Ishmael was. So the warning falls on all who rest in outward privilege — natural descent, ritual standing, a place in the visible church — without the sonship that the abiding Son alone confers. Privilege without faith, the verse implies, is a tenancy that can be ended.
36:
“If the Son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed.” The witnesses gather the argument of the preceding verses into this triumphant conclusion. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the word “indeed” (ontōs): the freedom the Son confers is “real,” “genuine,” true freedom, as opposed to the empty boast of v.33. Barnes explains that, since the Son abides in the house for ever (v.35) and has authority there, he alone can confer the standing of free sons; the liberty he gives is therefore not precarious like a slave’s but permanent and secure. Robertson and Ellicott note the connecting “therefore”: it follows from the Son’s eternal abiding that his act of liberation is lasting — whom the Son sets free “is free in reality and for ever.” The freedom is freedom from the slavery of sin (v.34), and through it the slave becomes a son.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that this freedom is found nowhere but in Christ.
