Whatever men imagine of their own liberty, there is “no true freedom but what the Son bestows”; and because it is the Son who bestows it, it is as sure and lasting as his own abiding. Wesley adds that the freedom is real and entire — not a change of masters but a true deliverance into the liberty of God’s children. The witnesses thus read the verse as the gospel answer to the whole dispute: against the Jews’ proud claim of a freedom they never truly had, Christ sets the only liberty that is real, the freedom from sin which the eternal Son alone can give, and which, once given, can never be lost.

The witnesses underline that the whole movement of the passage is from slavery to sonship. The slave of sin (v.34) has no abiding place (v.35); but the Son, who abides for ever, can make the slave free, and in freeing him make him a son who abides. So the liberty Christ promises is no mere release but an adoption: the freed bondsman is taken into the household for ever, sharing the permanence of the Son who set him free. This, the commentators agree, is the freedom the Jews’ boast could never reach.
37:
“I know that ye are Abraham’s seed; but ye seek to kill me, because my word hath no place in you.” The witnesses read this as Christ granting their descent while denying their kinship of spirit. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the concession and its limit: he “admits that you are the descendants of Abraham” (Barnes), but shows “they might be his descendants and still lack entirely his spirit” (cf. Matthew 3:9). The proof is their murderous intent (John 7:30) and the fact that his word “hath no place” in them — Robertson glosses the verb chōrei, “to have space or room for,” as “hath not free course”: “they had no longer room for his word when once they understood the spiritual aspect of his message,” so that “Jerusalem was now just like Galilee once before” (John 6:60–66). Ellicott notes he uses their own word “seed,” acknowledging “merely physical descent,” but “refuses to acknowledge that they are Abraham’s children” (v.39). Lightfoot hears the whole drift of the discourse pointing toward v.44: they are “the seed of that serpent that was to bruise the heel of the Messiah.”

The fathers and Reformers press the gravity of a word that finds no room. Chrysostom says Christ “reminds them of a present sin… actually at this moment in their thoughts,” loosening them “by degrees out of their relationship to Abraham.” Henry, for the Puritans, likens them to “a sick person who turns from his physician, and will take neither remedies nor food,” who is “past hope of recovery,” for “where the word of God has no place, no good is to be expected.” Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the concession as ridicule of their pride: granting the title they prize, “what avails it that those men are called Abraham’s seed, who are enraged against God and his ministers… and rush headlong to shed innocent blood?” The witnesses thus find the verse turning their proudest boast against them: descent from Abraham, with no room for Abraham’s God, leaves them not free sons but the serpent’s seed.
38:
“I speak that which I have seen with my Father: and ye do that which ye have seen with your father.” The witnesses take this as Christ tracing the deep difference between himself and his hearers to two opposite origins. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the two “fathers.” Robertson reads “with my Father” (para tō patri, locative) as “by the side of my Father,” a “picture of intimate fellowship” like the “face to face with God” of John 1:1, and insists that, though the same construction is used of “your father,” “ho patēr does not mean the same person — Christ’s Father by contrast is not their father.” Several note the better reading of the second clause: “ye do that which ye have heard with your father,” since (Clarke, with Bishop Pearce) “it could not be said… that the Jews had seen any thing with their father the devil, as it could that Jesus had seen with his.” Ellicott marks the “direct opposition” of the emphatic pronouns — “I, on My part… My Father; you, on your part… your father” — anticipating the naming of that father in v.44.

Henry, for the Puritans, makes the verse the watershed of the chapter: “here are two fathers spoken of, according to the two families into which the sons of men are divided — God and the devil — and without controversy these are contrary the one to the other.” Christ’s doctrine “was copied out of the counsels of infinite wisdom,” while their deeds were learned in another school. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as an argument “drawn from contrary things”: since Christ brings only “what I have learned from my Father,” their fury against his word proves they have “an opposite” father, and he thereby “protects the Gospel against contempt,” showing it no wonder if such men oppose it. The witnesses thus find the verse drawing the great Johannine line between the children of God and the children of the devil — a line traced not by blood but by the father whose works one does.
39:
“They answered and said unto him, Abraham is our father. Jesus saith unto them, If ye were Abraham’s children, ye would do the works of Abraham.” The witnesses note the Jews’ retreat to their genealogy and Christ’s sharp distinction between seed and children. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that, feeling the unspoken charge (v.44), they “claim the descent from Abraham which He has already allowed” (Ellicott). Christ answers by the principle that “the words sons and children are often used to denote those who imitate another or who have his spirit” (Barnes): “if you were worthy to be called the children of Abraham, or if you had his spirit,” you would “do the works of Abraham” — imitating him “in his faith, obedience, and uprightness” (Clarke). Ellicott sharpens it: “they are the physical seed of the patriarch, but they are not the ethical children, for the true child would bear the moral impress of the father.” Robertson notes the text-critical tangle — the better reading “if ye are” (a first-class condition) set against “ye would do” — but the sense is clear: their deeds belie their claim.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the exchange as proof of how “haughtily and fiercely they despised all Christ’s” teaching, clinging to a fleshly title while showing none of Abraham’s faith. The witnesses agree the verse lays bare the chapter’s governing principle: kinship in the things of God is moral and spiritual, not genealogical. To be Abraham’s child is to share Abraham’s faith and works; and the surest disproof of their boast is the murderous intent that Abraham, the friend of God, could never have owned. Their very answer — falling back on descent rather than character — confirms the charge it was meant to rebut.

The witnesses note the deftness of Christ’s reply: he neither denies their descent nor concedes their claim, but quietly shifts the test from blood to character. To be Abraham’s child, he says, is to do Abraham’s works — and so the appeal to genealogy, far from settling the matter, only sets up the standard by which they will be found wanting. The patriarch they invoke becomes the very measure that condemns them.
40:
“But now ye seek to kill me, a man that hath told you the truth, which I have heard of God: this did not Abraham.” The witnesses read this as Christ clinching the contrast between his hearers and the patriarch they claim. The grammatical-historical commentators note the plain logic: a true child of Abraham would imitate Abraham, but Abraham welcomed God’s messengers, whereas they “seek to kill” one “that hath told you the truth… heard of God” — “this did not Abraham” (Gill, Barnes). The deed disproves the descent: their murderous intent is the very opposite of Abraham’s reverent faith, so that, Ellicott notes, the conclusion already drawn in v.39 is now made unanswerable. Robertson observes that Jesus here calls himself “a man” (anthrōpon), describing his true humanity in the act of speaking the divine truth he had heard from God.

Cyril of Jerusalem seizes on that word for doctrine: “a man who has told you the truth” silences those “who speak against His humanity,” for Christ owns himself truly man even while claiming to bring the truth of God. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the heinousness the verse exposes: they are “not merely murderers,” but are “driven to such rage by hatred of God and his truth,” which is “far more heinous.” The witnesses thus find the verse completing the argument of descent: the works, not the blood, declare the father, and a people bent on killing the truthful messenger of God prove themselves, whatever their genealogy, no children of the friend of God but of another father altogether — the father whom the next verses will name.

The witnesses also mark the pathos of the phrase “a man that hath told you the truth.” Christ stands before them not as an accuser but as a truthful messenger, and their response to truth is murder — the surest sign of whose children they are. Abraham received the word of God with faith; they answer it with violence. The contrast, the commentators agree, is total, and it prepares the open naming of their true father in the verses that follow, toward which the whole argument has been moving.
41:
“Ye do the deeds of your father. Then said they to him, We be not born of fornication; we have one Father, even God.” The witnesses note that the unnamed father is now plainly in view, and that the Jews leap from Abraham to God. The grammatical-historical commentators observe Christ’s restraint: he “repeats the charge… as if unwilling to name Satan as their father,” choosing that “they should infer whom he meant” (Barnes). They, sensing “a spiritual father is intended” (Ellicott), abandon the appeal to Abraham and “claim God as their Father.” Their retort “We be not born of fornication” the commentators read against the prophetic use of fornication for idolatry (Isaiah 1:21; Jeremiah): they protest they are no idolatrous, spurious race but worship the one true God (Clarke, Ellicott). Robertson notes the proud boast, and the “vaguely possible” bitter edge — “an indirect fling at Jesus as the bastard son of Mary” — which Origen too detects, hearing them “insinuate that our Saviour was the offspring of adultery.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, exposes the false syllogism beneath their confidence: “God adopted for himself the family of Abraham; therefore, since we are Abraham’s descendants, we must be the children of God.” They “imagined that God was bound to the whole seed of Abraham,” supposing “they had holiness from the womb, because they were sprung from a holy root” — the very error, he adds, of “the Papists in the present day,” who vaunt their succession. The Latin Augustine and Cyril of Jerusalem press the principle that decides the matter: spiritual sonship is by imitation, not birth. As Paul became “the father of the Corinthians” by his teaching, so “the devil is called the father of those who of their own will consent unto him” (Cyril). The witnesses thus find the Jews’ boast — God their only Father — already refuted by the deeds that betray another paternity, which Christ is about to name outright.

The witnesses observe how the dialogue tightens like a net. Each claim the Jews advance — Abraham’s seed, then God their Father — Christ allows only to overturn it by their works, so that their genealogy and their religion alike become witnesses against them. The very vehemence with which they deny a lowly birth betrays how much they trusted in birth itself; and that trust, Calvin notes, is the root error from which their whole resistance to the gospel grows.
42:
“If God were your Father, ye would love me: for I proceeded forth and came from God.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s refutation of their claim to God by the test of love. The grammatical-historical commentators draw the logic out: God’s true children “would bear the spiritual image of their Father, and would love Him who came from God” (Ellicott), but they were seeking to kill him (v.40). Barnes makes it a chain: “all who truly love God, love his Son Jesus Christ,” for “every one that loveth him that begat, loveth him also that is begotten of him” (1 John 5:1); so those who “reject his Son… have no evidence that they are the friends of God.” Robertson notes the second-class condition “ye would love me,” which carries “the distinct implication that their failure to love Jesus is proof that God is not their Father,” and hears in “I proceeded forth from God” (exēlthon ek tou theou) “the definite consciousness of pre-existence with God” (John 17:5), the Incarnation being “the mission of the Son who proceeded from the Father” (Ellicott; cf. John 16:27).

Calvin, for the Reformed, frames the argument tightly: “whoever is a child of God will acknowledge his first-born Son; but you hate me, and therefore you have no reason to boast that you are God’s children.” He draws the abiding principle: “there is no piety and no fear of God where Christ is rejected,” and “how can they agree with the Father who disagree with his only Son?” Hypocritical religion may shelter “under the name of God,” but a knowledge of God “in which his lively image is rejected” is no knowledge at all. Wesley adds the compact note that Christ “proceeded forth as God, and came as Christ.” The witnesses thus make love of Christ the infallible mark of true sonship to God: to claim the Father while hating the Son is to refute oneself.

The witnesses press the searching application. If love of Christ is the test of true sonship to God, then no profession of religion, however zealous, can stand where that love is absent. The Jews’ hatred of Jesus is thus not a side-issue but the disproof of their deepest claim; and the same test, the commentators note, falls on every age — that those who reject the Son, whatever their reverence for God’s name, show thereby that they are not his children.
43:
“Why do ye not understand my speech? even because ye cannot hear my word.” The witnesses distinguish the two terms and expose the root of the Jews’ incomprehension. The grammatical-historical commentators preserve the distinction between “speech” (lalia, the manner or form of words) and “word” (logos, the matter spoken): Ellicott and Robertson agree that they “will not listen to the substance of Christ’s teaching, and hence they are impatient with the way that he talks.” The key is the sense of “cannot hear.” Barnes explains that “hear” here means “bear or tolerate,” as in John 6:60: “his doctrine was offensive to them. They hated it, and hence they perverted his meaning.” Clarke makes it pointed: his doctrine “comes too close to you; it searches your hearts, detects your hypocrisy… and as ye are determined not to leave your sins, so ye are purposed not to hear my doctrine.” Lightfoot illustrates from Isaiah 11:4 — “he shall smite the earth with the rod of his mouth” — for “they could not bear the smart of his rod” and so “would not understand the phraseology he used.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as a charge of obstinacy so great that they are “actuated and hurried away by diabolical rage.” Wesley draws the same conclusion and ties it to the chapter’s earlier principle: “such is your stubbornness and pride… not being desirous to do my will, ye cannot understand my doctrine” (John 7:17). The witnesses thus locate the failure not in the obscurity of Christ’s words but in the hostility of his hearers: the inability to understand is itself a moral inability, a refusal to bear a truth that condemns them. Their misunderstanding from v.33 onward is shown to be willful, the deafness of a heart that will not hear.

The witnesses draw the sobering lesson that unbelief is finally a matter of the will, not the intellect. Christ’s words were plain enough; what the Jews lacked was not light but the willingness to receive a light that exposed them. So their question, “Why do we not understand?”, is answered by a deeper diagnosis than they expected: they do not understand because they will not bear to — the surest proof that the fault lay not in the Teacher but in themselves.
44:
“Ye are of your father the devil, and the lusts of your father ye will do.” The witnesses receive this as the chapter’s thunderclap, and the fathers at once guard it from misuse. The Eastern Chrysostom notes that Christ, “having already cut off the Jews from relationship to Abraham, overthrows now this far greater claim, to call God their Father.” But Augustine and Origen insist the sonship is moral, not natural: against “the heresy of the Manicheans, who hold a certain original nature of evil,” Augustine declares “the Jews were children of the devil by imitation, not by birth” — “you are his children… because you have such lusts, not because you are born of him.” To read the verse as making them evil by nature, drawn “from the opposite seed of darkness,” is the very Manichean error the orthodox reject.

On the devil’s two marks — murder and lying — the witnesses are agreed. He “was a murderer from the beginning,” which Augustine refers to the first man, slain not by sword but when the devil “sowed an evil word, and slew him”; and Barnes and Robertson note the strong word anthrōpoktonos (man-slayer), pointing to the ruin of Adam and the murder of Abel (Genesis 3; Genesis 4:8; cf. 1 John 3:12). He “abode not in the truth,” and “when he speaketh a lie, he speaketh of his own: for he is a liar, and the father of it.” Henry, for the Puritans, gathers the devil’s lusts as “spiritual wickedness… pride and envy, wrath and malice, enmity to good, and enticing others to evil,” all carried on “by calling evil good, and good evil.” Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that the Jews’ murderous hatred of the truth marks them as the children of the liar and murderer — not by a fate of nature, but by the works in which they freely imitate him. The witnesses thus read the verse as the terrible obverse of the gospel: as the Son makes free sons of God, so unbelief, hatred of the truth, and bloodthirst stamp men as children of the devil, whose lusts they choose to do.
45:
“And because I tell you the truth, ye believe me not.” The witnesses dwell on the startling reversal this verse names: truth itself becoming the cause of unbelief. The grammatical-historical commentators bring out the emphatic contrast. Ellicott reads the pronoun as “full of emphatic meaning — I, as contrary to him,” the devil, “because I tell you the truth, as contrary to the lie which is his characteristic.” He marks how arresting this is: “to speak the truth is commonly to command belief… the mind of man is so constituted that truth is the first object of its search,” yet “here was perfect truth presented to men, and they refused to accept it.” Robertson notes the proleptic, emphatic “I” and quotes Bernard: “truth is uncongenial to them,” pointing back to the verdict of John 3:19 — men loving darkness rather than light. Gill specifies the truth they reject as “the whole truth of the Gospel, and particularly the truth of his divine sonship.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse confirm the preceding charge: “since they have no other reason for opposing, but because truth is hateful and intolerable to them, they show plainly that they are the children of Satan.” The point, the witnesses agree, is diagnostic. Ordinarily a hearer disbelieves because he doubts a claim is true; here the Jews disbelieve precisely because the claim is true, their hostility proof of a heart at enmity with truth itself. So Christ’s very faithfulness — telling them not flattering lies but the searching truth — becomes, in their hands, the occasion of their rejection, and thereby the clearest evidence of whose children they are. The verse thus stands as a mirror for every age: where truth provokes not belief but rage, the fault lies not in the truth but in the corrupt will that cannot bear it.

The witnesses press how the verse seals the indictment of the preceding ones. Having traced their unbelief to a father whose nature is falsehood (v.44), Christ now shows its symptom: they cannot abide the truth. A heart at home with the liar will find the truth-teller intolerable, and so the very perfection of Christ’s honesty becomes the stone over which they stumble. Their rejection, far from telling against his claim, is exactly what the gospel would predict of children of the father of lies.
46:
“Which of you convinceth me of sin? And if I say the truth, why do ye not believe me?” The witnesses divide over the sense of “sin” here, and unite on the force of the challenge. The first question Robertson renders “which of you convicteth me of sin?” — the verb elenchō meaning to charge and prove (cf. John 16:8) — and takes “sin” “as in John 1:29” to mean “sin in general,” so that the unanswered question “involves sinlessness” (Hebrews 4:15) “without specifically saying so.” Ellicott agrees it is an appeal “to their knowledge of His sinless life” (v.29), “an appeal that spotless purity alone could make”; he pictures a pause after the challenge, during which “no one of all who had watched Him in Galilee and Judæa dared utter a syllable” — “their silence is the seal” of his innocence. But Barnes and Clarke press a narrower sense: since “sin” here “stands opposed to truth” in the same verse, it should be rendered “error, falsehood, or imposture” — “which of you can prove that I am an impostor?” On this reading the argument is that a doctrine may be rejected only if its teacher is shown to be a deceiver, and they cannot show it.

Calvin, for the Reformed, hears “perfect confidence”: knowing “they could not justly bring any reproach against him, he glories over his enemies, as having obtained a victory” — not that he was free from their slanders, but that “no crime dwells in him.” Wesley draws the practical point: “is not my life as unreprovable as my doctrine? Does not my whole behaviour confirm the truth of what I teach?” The witnesses thus find in the verse both a unique self-witness and an unanswerable argument: the sinless (or, on the other reading, the guileless) Teacher binds his hearers to believe, since they can convict him of neither sin nor fraud, and their unbelief is therefore, as Robertson says, sheer irrationality “based on prejudice and predilection.”

The witnesses also note the boldness of so public a challenge. No mere man could stand before lifelong enemies, who had watched his every step, and dare them to name a single sin; yet Jesus does, and the silence that answers is itself a testimony. Whether the word means moral fault or fraud, the effect is the same: they can convict him of neither, and so their refusal to believe is left without excuse, resting, as Robertson says, on nothing but prejudice.
47:
“He that is of God heareth God’s words: ye therefore hear them not, because ye are not of God.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s answer to his own question of v.46 — why they do not believe — and the counterpart to the dread verdict of v.44. The grammatical-historical commentators draw the symmetry: as the children of the devil do the devil’s lusts, so “he who is of God does the will of God, and hears the words of God” (Ellicott). To “hear,” Barnes explains, is to “listen to, or attend to the doctrines… as a child who loves his parent will regard and obey,” and “a willingness to receive all that God teaches… is an undoubted proof that we are his friends” (1 John 2:4). Robertson, with Dods, makes the inference plain: “their not listening proved that they were not of God.” Gill grounds the “being of God” in the new birth — born “not of blood… but of God… by the power of his grace.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the verse as Christ’s confident conclusion. Taking it “for granted that he is the ambassador of the heavenly Father,” and having shown that he taught nothing “but as from the mouth of God,” he “concludes that they have nothing in common with God, for they do not hear the words of God.” The witnesses thus find here a searching test of true relationship to God, cutting beneath all outward profession: not descent, nor office, nor zeal, but the glad hearing of God’s word marks his children. The Jews’ deafness to that word — “Rabbis and priests, teachers of the Law… offerers of sacrifice” though they were (Ellicott) — is therefore the proof that, for all their religion, they were not of God. The same test, the commentators imply, still divides hearers in every congregation.

The witnesses underline the searching edge of the test. It is not enough to be a teacher of the Law or an offerer of sacrifice; the mark of belonging to God is the glad reception of his word. By that measure the most religious of men may be shown to be strangers to God, and the verse therefore unsettles every false confidence, throwing the hearer back upon the one question that matters — whether he truly hears, and loves, the word of God when it is spoken.
48:
“Then answered the Jews… Say we not well that thou art a Samaritan, and hast a devil?” The witnesses agree this is the eruption of malice when argument fails. The Eastern Chrysostom observes that “a shameless and a forward thing is wickedness, and when it ought to hide itself, then is it the fiercer”: when they should have been “pricked” and won by “the boldness and conclusiveness of the words,” they instead “insult Him.” The grammatical-historical commentators explain the two slurs. “Samaritan,” Barnes and Clarke note, was a term of contempt equivalent to “heretic” or “schismatic,” one “unworthy of any credit” (cf. John 4:9); Robertson says they knew he was no Samaritan but charged it because “he had acted like a Samaritan in challenging their peculiar spiritual privileges.” The charge of “hast a devil” — “art possessed… and art, in consequence, deranged” (Clarke) — was, Robertson notes, “an old one” (Matthew 12:24) and would be repeated (John 10:20). Lightfoot exposes the insincerity: would they really suffer a Samaritan to stand “in the Treasury” (v.20)? — proof the slur was “spoken in rancour and mere malice,” they “perfectly knowing that he was no Samaritan.”

The Latin Augustine turns the scene to instruction: “from power we learn patience.” Christ, “the Just One” and “the Creator,” bore “Thou hast a devil” without retaliation; “let him first imitate His patience” who would attain his power. Henry, for the Puritans, marks that those who now fall “to downright railing” were “the men of consequence,” the scribes and Pharisees, who, “convicted of an obstinate infidelity, scornfully turned off the conviction” with abuse. Calvin, for the Reformed, sees them “stupefied by Satan,” so desperate that, “fully convicted, still they are enraged.” The witnesses thus read the railing as the confession of a beaten cause: unable to answer the truth or convict its Teacher of sin, they can only revile him — and in reviling the sinless One as devil-possessed, they betray once more whose children they are.

The witnesses note the irony that, in calling the sinless One a demoniac, the Jews unwittingly repeat the sin Christ had just exposed. Unable to convict him of any fault (v.46) or to answer his reasoning, they resort to the weapon of the father of lies — slander. Their railing is thus not a refutation but a confession; the very abuse they hurl is the mark of the paternity he has charged upon them, and it leaves his argument wholly untouched.
49:
“Jesus answered, I have not a devil; but I honour my Father, and ye do dishonour me.” The witnesses note both what Christ answers and what he passes over. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that he “takes no notice of the first charge,” the name “Samaritan,” because it was “so notorious” that he was not one (Gill); it was, besides, a mere term of abuse. He denies only the graver slander, “I have not a devil,” and meets it not with counter-railing but with the calm assertion of his true relation: “I honour my Father.” The contrast is pointed — “I honour my Father, and ye do dishonour me”: so far from being demon-driven, all his work is the honouring of God, while their abuse of him is, by implication, a dishonouring of the Father who sent him. Robertson and Ellicott note the dignity and restraint of the reply, which answers calumny with the simple statement of fact.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the logic beneath the meekness: in honouring the Father, Christ does the very opposite of what a devil would do, so the charge refutes itself; and in dishonouring him, the Jews dishonour the God they profess to defend. The witnesses thus find in the verse a model of holy patience joined to unflinching truth. Christ neither trades insult for insult nor abandons his claim; he lets the false charge fall by simply denying it, and turns at once to the one thing that governs all his conduct — the honour of his Father — leaving their dishonour to expose itself. The brief answer prepares the great words that follow, in which he will set his own honour wholly in the Father’s hands.

The witnesses observe how the answer turns the accusation inside out. The Jews had charged him with serving a devil; he replies that his whole life is the honouring of his Father — the work most opposite to a devil’s. And the dishonour they heap on him recoils upon themselves, for to revile the One the Father sent is to insult the Father. So the meek denial carries a quiet counter-charge, and prepares the way for Christ to commit his honour, and theirs, to the judgment of God.
50:
“And I seek not mine own glory: there is one that seeketh and judgeth.” The witnesses read this as Christ committing his vindication to the Father. The grammatical-historical commentators stress his self-denial: “in all his teaching this was true,” Barnes says — “he did not seek to exalt or to vindicate himself,” being “willing to lie under reproach and to be despised,” and so untroubled by their taunts. Ellicott notes the words “repeat what He had taught them before” (John 5:41; John 7:18): “for Him whose whole life was one of self-denial, their dishonour finds nothing which it can wound.” On “there is one that seeketh,” the commentators are agreed: it is the Father who “seeketh for the honour of the Son, and judgeth between Him and those who dishonour Him” (Ellicott; cf. John 5:45). Robertson adds the careful balance — though “the Son is the Judge of mankind” (John 5:22), here “the Father judges between you and me” — and quotes Bernard: “it is only the glory that comes from God that is worth having.”

Gill grounds it in Christ’s confidence that “Wisdom was justified of her children,” so that “he committed himself to God that judgeth righteously, who would take care of his glory, and vindicate him.” Clarke and Wesley hear in “there is one that… judgeth” a quiet warning: the Father who seeks the Son’s glory “will pass sentence on you accordingly” for your obstinacy. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a model and a comfort for the persecuted servant of God: not to grasp at self-vindication, nor to be cast down by slander, but to leave one’s honour in the hands of the Father who both seeks it and judges righteously between his servant and the world. Christ’s serenity under reproach flows from this — that his glory is safe with God.

The witnesses note how this composure rebukes the self-seeking of the Jews, who in 5:44 sought glory one from another. Christ, by contrast, is content to be reviled, certain that the Father will both seek his honour and judge his cause. The verse thus models the disposition of every true servant of God: to labour for God’s glory rather than one’s own, and to entrust one’s reputation to the righteous Judge rather than to wrest it from the hands of men.
51:
“Verily, verily, I say unto you, If a man keep my saying, he shall never see death.” The witnesses receive this solemn promise as the heart of the chapter’s offer of life. The grammatical-historical commentators define both the condition and the gift. To “keep my word” (Robertson notes the better reading, a third-class condition) is, Barnes says, to “believe on me and obey my commandments” — the phrase recurring through John (John 14:23; 1 John 2:5) and equivalent to keeping his commands. “Never see death” is a Hebraism (Psalm 89:48) for never dying, and means here, all agree, spiritual and eternal death: “he shall obtain eternal life” (Barnes; cf. John 5:24; John 11:25). Ellicott makes John 5:24 the key — “he that heareth my word… passeth from death unto life” — the believer possessing now “the true spiritual life which cannot see death.” Clarke draws the contrast with Moses, who “promised a long life” to the law-keeper, whereas Christ promises that the keeper of his word “shall never come under the power of the death of the soul, but shall live eternally.”

Henry, for the Puritans, calls it “the doctrine of the immortality of believers,” and unfolds “the character of a believer” as one who keeps Christ’s word — receiving it, holding it “in mind and memory… in love and affection.” Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the verse as Christ’s deliberate turn from threatening to comfort: knowing “some persons in that multitude were curable,” he “intended to terrify the wicked… but in such a manner as to leave ground of consolation for the good,” so that “the faithful teacher ought not to be wholly employed in reproving the wicked, but ought also to impart the doctrine of salvation to the children of God.” Wesley adds that we keep his word “by believing his promises… by obeying his command,” and that the promise of deathlessness itself “proves that he was no Samaritan, for the Samaritans were Sadducees.” The witnesses thus find in this verse the gracious counterpart to the chapter’s severity — the offer of life that never dies to all who keep the word of Christ.
52:
“Then said the Jews unto him, Now we know that thou hast a devil. Abraham is dead, and the prophets; and thou sayest, If a man keep my saying, he shall never taste of death.” The witnesses note the Jews’ fresh blasphemy and the literalism beneath it. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that, taking Christ’s words “in a merely physical sense” (Ellicott), they find them flatly “contradicted by the fact that death came to the great Patriarch and the prophets,” and conclude that “no one, except a man under the influence of a demon, would make an assertion so opposed to the almost unbroken experience of mankind.” Robertson marks the perfect tense “now we know” (egnōkamen), “a state of completion” — “now since such talk we have come to certain knowledge that thou hast a demon” — and notes they subtly shift his “see death” to “taste of death.” Origen observes that they reckoned Christ to speak “by the influence of the devil… because they did not know the power of God.”

The fathers supply the deeper irony. Gregory remarks that “as it is necessary that the good should grow better by contumely, so are the reprobate made worse by kindness” — the very promise of life provoking their blasphemy. Theophylact and Augustine, looking ahead to v.55, read “I keep his saying” of the Son’s unchanging unity with the Father. Calvin, for the Reformed, exposes their error precisely: they “apply the spiritual doctrine of Christ to the body,” whereas “no believer ever dies” in the sense Christ means, “because believers, born of incorruptible seed (1 Peter 1:23), live even when they die,” being united to him. The witnesses thus find the Jews condemned out of their own literalism: dragging a promise of eternal life down to the level of the body, they call the Author of life a demoniac, and so prove again how the same word that saves the believing hardens the reprobate.

The witnesses observe that the Jews, in their confidence, suppose themselves to have caught Christ in an absurdity, when in truth they have only displayed their own deafness to spiritual things. Abraham and the prophets did die in the body, but they live unto God; and the very promise the Jews deride is the hope in which the fathers themselves rested. Their triumphant ‘now we know’ is thus the surest proof of how little they know — measuring the words of the Lord of life by the bare experience of the grave.
53:
“Art thou greater than our father Abraham, which is dead? and the prophets are dead: whom makest thou thyself?” The witnesses hear in this both the old Jewish pride and a half-glimpsed suspicion of blasphemy. The grammatical-historical commentators note the question echoes the Samaritan woman’s about Jacob (John 4:12), with Abraham substituted; Robertson observes the form expects the answer “no” — “surely thou art not greater than our father Abraham” — and that “they do not see that Jesus uses death in a different sense.” Barnes catches the scorn: that “a Nazarene, a Samaritan, and a devil” should claim to keep his followers from dying was a question “implying… contempt and scorn.” Yet the sharper edge is in “whom makest thou thyself?” — a predicate accusative by which, Robertson notes, “they suspect that Jesus is guilty of blasphemy as they charged in John 5:18,” a charge they will later make explicit (John 10:33; John 19:7). Ellicott frames their reasoning: “if Abraham… himself died, and if the prophets… themselves died, what kind of person dost Thou assert Thyself to be that Thy word shall deliver men from death?”

Calvin, for the Reformed, names the offence beneath the question: they “endeavour to obscure the glory of Christ by the splendour of Abraham and of the saints.” But “as all the stars are thrown into the shade by the brightness of the sun, so all the glory… in all the saints must fade away before the incomparable brightness of Christ”; to contrast “the servants with the Lord” is to “act unjustly and absurdly,” and to abuse the very name of the fathers “in opposition to Christ.” The witnesses thus find the Jews stumbling, without knowing it, over the true answer: he is indeed greater than Abraham and the prophets, the Lord whose word gives the life that even the patriarchs awaited — a greatness the next verses will declare in the most startling terms.

The witnesses note the unconscious aptness of the question. ‘Whom makest thou thyself?’ is asked in scorn, yet it touches the very point at issue: not whom Christ pretends to be, but who he truly is. He makes himself nothing, as the next verse will say; it is the Father who makes him great. And the answer they could not bear is precisely the one the chapter is moving toward — that he is greater than Abraham because, before Abraham was, he is.
54:
“Jesus answered, If I honour myself, my honour is nothing: it is my Father that honoureth me; of whom ye say, that he is your God.” The witnesses read this as Christ once more referring his glory to the Father, and exposing the emptiness of the Jews’ claim on God. The grammatical-historical commentators note the principle, already stated at John 5:31: self-sought honour “is of no value” (Barnes), “empty and vain” (Gill). His true honour comes from another — “it is my Father that honoureth me” — so that, in answering “whom makest thou thyself?”, Christ replies that he makes himself nothing, but is made glorious by the Father (cf. John 17:1). The sting lies in the closing clause: the Father who honours him is the very God “of whom ye say, that he is your God” — a God they claim but, as the next verse will say, do not know. Clarke notes the textual variation between “your God” and “our God,” but the point holds either way.

Henry, for the Puritans, and Calvin, for the Reformed, press the irony. The Jews boast of God as theirs while dishonouring the Son he honours; their religion is loud in profession and empty in reality. Calvin observes that Christ “does not simply claim glory for himself, but ascribes it wholly to the Father,” and thereby convicts them of a hollow piety — claiming God with their lips while rejecting the One whom God exalts. The witnesses thus find in the verse a double movement: Christ’s perfect humility, refusing all self-glory and resting in the Father’s honour; and the unmasking of his accusers, whose proud claim “he is your God” is about to be answered with the charge that they have never truly known him. The boast of God on their lips becomes the measure of their condemnation.

The witnesses note how exactly this answers the Jews’ taunt. They had asked whom he made himself; he replies that he makes himself nothing, claiming no honour but what the Father gives. So the charge of self-exaltation falls to the ground, and rebounds upon them: it is they who exalt themselves in claiming a God they do not know, while the One they accuse seeks only the Father’s glory. The contrast between true and false honour is laid bare, and with it the emptiness of a religion that boasts in God yet rejects his Son.
55:
“Yet ye have not known him; but I know him: and if I should say, I know him not, I shall be a liar like unto you: but I know him, and keep his saying.” The witnesses take this as the sharp climax of the contrast between Christ and his accusers. The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the two halves. On the Jews’ side, their boast “he is your God” (v.54) is hollow, for “ye have not known him”; their claim to God is contradicted by their rejection of the Son whom God sent, so that they are revealed as ignorant of the very God they profess. On Christ’s side stands the calm, unqualified “but I know him” — a knowledge, Robertson and Ellicott note, that is immediate and perfect, the knowledge of the Son who is from the Father. The middle clause presses the point with startling force: were Christ to deny that knowledge, “I shall be a liar like unto you” — that is, to disclaim his unique knowledge of the Father, to dissemble out of false modesty, would make him as false as they. Truth itself compels him to assert what they count blasphemy.

Gill marks that Christ adds the proof of his knowledge — “and keep his saying” — for to know the Father and to keep his word are inseparable; the Son’s obedience attests his knowledge. The witnesses thus find the verse setting an unbridgeable distance between the two parties: the accusers, loud in their claim on God yet strangers to him; and the accused, who knows the Father with a knowledge so certain that to deny it would be the one lie he could not tell. Far from the demoniac boaster they have called him, Christ stands as the truthful witness whose very honesty requires him to confess the intimacy with God that his hearers lack — and which the following verses will trace back before Abraham himself.

The witnesses press the boldness of the middle clause. Christ will not, even to soften their fury, pretend to less than he is; to disown his knowledge of the Father would be the one falsehood incompatible with his nature as the Truth. So the same honesty that the Jews counted blasphemy is in fact the proof of his integrity — he speaks the unwelcome truth of his unique knowledge of God precisely because he cannot lie. Here, as throughout the chapter, truthfulness itself divides him utterly from the children of the father of lies.
56:
“Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day: and he saw it, and was glad.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s answer to “art thou greater than Abraham?” (v.53) — turning the patriarch they claimed into a witness against them. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word “rejoiced” (ēgalliasato): it “includes the notion of desire as well as rejoicing” (Barnes), and Clarke pictures the soul of Abraham leaping “forward in earnest hope and strong expectation,” like one who runs to meet a long-awaited friend. Robertson links it to Hebrews 11:13, the fathers “saluting the promises from afar.” On how Abraham “saw” the day, the commentators offer several modes: by faith in the promise that in his seed all nations should be blessed (Genesis 22:18; Ellicott, Wesley), in types and theophanies such as the appearance at Mamre (Genesis 18:1; Wesley), and perhaps in the vision of Genesis 15 (Robertson notes a Jewish tradition that Abraham then saw his descendants’ whole history). Ellicott draws the contrast sharply: Abraham “realised the fulness of the promises… exulting” to see the Messiah from afar, while these, his supposed children, reject the Messiah “actually among them.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the contrast the heart of the verse: Abraham “had no other object during his whole life than to see my kingdom flourish; he longed for me when I was absent, you despise me when I am present.” And what is true of Abraham “applies to all the saints,” though “with greater weight in the person of Abraham, because he is the father of the whole Church”: “whoever desires to be reckoned in the number of the godly, let him rejoice… in the presence of Christ, for which Abraham ardently longed.” Cyril of Jerusalem turns it to the Son’s eternity, citing Micah 5:2 — his “goings forth are from… the days of eternity.” The witnesses thus find the verse both honouring Abraham’s forward-looking faith and condemning the Jews: the father they boast of welcomed the very Christ they would stone.

The witnesses observe how the verse quietly establishes Christ’s superiority to Abraham, which the Jews had challenged. Abraham is great, but his greatness lay in longing for and welcoming the day of Christ; the patriarch was the servant who awaited his Lord. To set Abraham above Christ, then, is to mistake the worshipper for the One worshipped — and the chapter is about to show, in the plainest words, just how far above Abraham the Son in truth stands.
57:
“Then said the Jews unto him, Thou art not yet fifty years old, and hast thou seen Abraham?” The witnesses note the carnal literalism of the objection and a perversion of Christ’s words. Gregory observes that “the carnal minds of the Jews are intent on the flesh only; they think only of His age in the flesh,” reckoning the centuries since Abraham died. The grammatical-historical commentators add that they have twisted his claim: Christ said Abraham saw his day, but they retort, “hast thou seen Abraham?” (Barnes). On the round number “fifty,” the commentators differ: Jesus was about thirty-three (Theophylact, Gill), so some ask why not “forty.” Robertson calls fifty “the crisis of completed manhood” (Numbers 4:3) and “a round number”; Lightfoot ties it to the age of the Levites’ superannuation (Numbers 4); Gill suggests his face, “a man of sorrows,” made him look older. Ellicott defends the reading “fifty” against the ancient substitution of “forty,” noting that some Elders who “conferred with John” (reported by Irenaeus) even held Jesus to have been between forty and fifty. Robertson records the textual variant by which some manuscripts read “hath Abraham seen thee?”

Calvin, for the Reformed, sees them trying “to refute Christ’s saying by showing that he had asserted what was impossible.” They allow him to be “somewhat older” than he was, “that they may not appear too rigid,” as if to say, “Thou certainly wilt not make thyself so old, though thou wert to boast.” He dismisses the notion of Papias that Christ lived more than forty years. The witnesses thus find the Jews, fixed on the flesh, walking straight into the answer they cannot conceive: their scornful arithmetic — fifty years against two thousand — measures only the man they see, and leaves them wholly unprepared for the claim of eternal being that Christ is about to make. Their question about age is about to be answered by a word that lifts him clean out of time.

The witnesses note the deeper irony. Confident that they have exposed an impossibility, the Jews have in fact set the stage for the most stupendous claim of the Gospel. They reckon by years and decades; Christ is about to answer in the language of eternity. Their very objection — measuring him against the centuries since Abraham — invites the reply that he is before Abraham altogether, the timeless ‘I am’ whom no reckoning of years can contain.
58:
“Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am.” The witnesses are unanimous that here Christ claims eternal, divine being. The grammatical-historical commentators ground it in the very grammar. Robertson is emphatic: the contrast between “before Abraham came into existence” (prin Abraham genesthai) and the timeless “I am” (egō eimi) is “complete” — “undoubtedly here Jesus claims eternal existence with the absolute phrase used of God.” Ellicott draws the same line: “Abraham, like all men, came into being. There was a time when he was not. But there was never a time when the Son of God was not… He is the I AM, present equally in the human ‘was,’ and ‘is,’ and ‘is to come’” (cf. John 1:1). Barnes notes that the present tense “I am,” applied to God, “denotes continued existence without respect to time,” as in “from everlasting to everlasting thou art God” (Psalm 90:2). Clarke, following Calmet, paraphrases: “I am from all eternity… besides this human nature… there is in me a Divine and eternal nature,” which Abraham “adored… as his God, and desired… as his Savior.” Lightfoot notes that the Jews had perverted the question — Christ spoke of Abraham seeing his day, they of his seeing Abraham — and that he answers on a wholly higher plane.

The word inevitably recalls the divine Name “I AM THAT I AM” (Exodus 3:14), the same self-designation that has echoed through the chapter (vv.24, 28). Calvin, for the Reformed, and Wesley both take the words as the plainest assertion of Christ’s eternal Godhead. The witnesses thus find the discourse reaching its summit: to the scornful question how one not yet fifty could have seen Abraham, Christ answers not that he is older than Abraham but that he is before and above all time — the eternal “I am,” who simply is while Abraham only “came to be.” It is the most direct claim of deity in the Gospel, and the Jews, as the next verse shows, understand it exactly so.
59:
“Then took they up stones to cast at him: but Jesus hid himself, and went out of the temple, going through the midst of them, and so passed by.” The witnesses read the stones as proof that the Jews understood the great claim exactly. The grammatical-historical commentators note that they reached for stones because they heard in “Before Abraham was, I am” the blasphemy of a man making himself God (the penalty for which was stoning, Leviticus 24:16); Gill and Lightfoot add that the temple, then still under construction, lay ready with building-stones to hand. That their fury was kindled by the claim of deity, the commentators agree, confirms the sense of v.58 — they took it as no less than a claim to be God. The withdrawal, Robertson and Ellicott note, is told simply: “Jesus hid himself” (ekrybē) and passed out through their midst, his hour not yet come (cf. John 7:30).

The Latin fathers find in the scene both doctrine and mystery. Augustine fastens on the grammar he has been pressing: Christ “did not say, before Abraham was, but, before Abraham was made,” for Abraham is “a creature,” while of the Word it stands written “in the beginning WAS the Word”; nor does he say “I was made,” because he simply is. Gregory adds that “their unbelieving minds were unable to support these indications of eternity, and, not understanding Him, sought to destroy Him.” Augustine reads even the withdrawal tenderly: having “done all that He could do as a teacher,” and meeting only stones, “He leaves them,” yet “did not hide Himself” as one afraid, but as one whose time was not yet. The witnesses thus close the chapter on its sharpest contrast: the eternal “I am” offering life and light, and the children of the father of murder answering with stones — and the One they cannot seize passing untouched through their midst, master still of the hour of his death.

The witnesses draw the chapter’s closing lesson from the violence and the escape together. That the Jews answered a claim of grace and life with stones lays bare the murderous spirit Christ had charged upon them (v.44); and that he passed unharmed through their midst shows that, even in the face of death, he remained sovereign over his own hour. The Light of the world, rejected and assailed, withdraws — not defeated, but in command — leaving his hearers to the darkness they have chosen.
